findings.title: Collate holds every sidecar text in RAM 3x (list-of-dicts -> DataFrame -> merge -> arrow) before one giant to_parquet; stream slabs to a ParquetWriter instead
findings.description: Stage 12 accumulates one Python dict per audio file containing ALL sidecar contents (~17 columns: accent/rover/punct/phonemes + per-model transcripts + .tst timestamp blobs) into `results`, then builds `extracted_df = pd.DataFrame(results)` (line 290), pd.merge with the metadata frame (292), runs consistency, and finally `to_parquet` which converts the whole frame to Arrow — yet another full copy of every string. At 2M rows: dict overhead alone is ~1.5-2.2 GB (one ~1.1 KB dict per row), sidecar text content (transcripts ~0.2-1 KB x ~8 models + multi-KB .tst JSON) is ~5-10 GB as Python strings, and `results` is never freed, so peak = dicts + strings + object-frame pointers + a second full copy of all string data in Arrow buffers inside to_parquet. That plausibly exceeds the ~21 GB free on this node and OOMs at production scale. The consistency step adds its own transient: lines 145-151 materialize k normalized copies of every transcript plus a concat of all of them before factorize (~2x all-transcript bytes). All of this is row-independent: consistency depends only on the row's own transcripts, sidecar reads are per-file, and `executor.map` already yields slabs in input order.
findings.proposed_fix: Stream the export: pre-index metadata once (df.set_index('filepath') or a dict), then for each 100-200k-row slab of audio_paths collect sidecar columns into per-column lists (no per-row dicts), join metadata by lookup, compute asr_consistency on the slab (factorize per slab gives identical results since agreement is within-row), build a pyarrow Table and append via pyarrow.parquet.ParquetWriter with a fixed schema. Row order is preserved (slabs are ordered), so values and order are identical; only row-group layout/file bytes differ. While there, expose `parquet_compression` (zstd shrinks text-heavy output ~2x = fewer HDD write bytes) and replace the `.str.split().str.join(' ')` normalization (allocates a word-list per row) with `.str.replace(r'\s+',' ',regex=True).str.strip()` — same ' '.join(s.split()) semantics. Peak RAM drops from O(dataset text) to O(slab + metadata frame). No numba/C++ needed — this is allocation discipline, not compute.
findings.expected_gain: Peak RAM at 2M rows drops from an estimated 12-20 GB (OOM territory on this 31 GB node) to ~2-3 GB; wall-clock roughly neutral to slightly faster (giant merge replaced by ordered slab joins; ParquetWriter writes overlap collection). Scales with dataset size — at 10M rows the current code cannot run on this node at all.
findings.risk: medium — parquet VALUES and row order provably identical (pin with a round-trip equality test like tests/test_collate_consistency.py), but file bytes/row-group layout differ from the single-shot to_parquet; schema must be fixed up-front (all sidecar cols are str, consistency float). Keep the old path behind a knob if byte-stability of the .parquet matters.

findings.title: to_webdataset still pickles the FULL metadata_dict to every worker — report.md §8 claims this was fixed, but it is not in the code
findings.description: report.md §8 states 'webdataset workers now receive only their shard's metadata instead of the full dict', but `executor.submit(worker_fn, worker_id, chunk, wds_output_dir, metadata_dict, ...)` at to_webdataset.py:212 ships the ENTIRE metadata_dict to every ProcessPoolExecutor worker. The commit 5c124e5 that report.md cites only vectorized load_metadata and the sibling scandir — the subsetting was never applied. At 2M rows the records dict is ~2-4 GB (one ~1 KB dict per stem plus values); with export.num_workers raised above the config default of 1, the parent pickles those GB once per worker (tens of seconds each, serialized in the parent) and each child unpickles a full private copy: 8 workers x ~3 GB = guaranteed OOM on this 31 GB node. This is exactly the trap flagged in .claude/analysis/denoising-export.json ('to_webdataset metadata_dict pickling') — still open.
findings.proposed_fix: Subset per chunk before submit: `stems = {os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(p))[0] for p in chunk}; chunk_meta = {s: metadata_dict[s] for s in stems if s in metadata_dict}` and pass chunk_meta. ~6 lines, outputs identical (each worker only ever looks up its own chunk's stems). Do NOT rely on fork COW — the per-cell sanitization loop touches refcounts and un-shares the pages anyway. Also fix report.md §8 to match reality. Optionally split into more chunks than workers (chunk_id in the shard pattern instead of worker_id) to fix byte-skew stragglers, since chunks are count-balanced, not byte-balanced.
findings.expected_gain: Removes the N-workers RAM multiplier (8 workers: ~24 GB -> ~3 GB total for metadata) and N full-dict pickle/unpickle cycles at pool startup (minutes at 2M rows). Makes num_workers > 1 actually usable on this node.
findings.risk: low — byte-identical shards (same keys looked up); trivial change. The report.md correction is documentation-only.

findings.title: Collate loads balalaika.csv with plain pd.read_csv instead of the pyarrow fast_read_csv used everywhere else
findings.description: Every other CSV consumer (to_webdataset.py:54, csv_manager users) goes through `fast_read_csv` (multithreaded pyarrow parser, ~4x faster, BALALAIKA_CSV_ENGINE=c escape hatch). Collate's main-CSV load at line 244 is still `pd.read_csv(df_path)` — the single biggest CSV read in the stage. Per report.md §4.1's own measurements the pyarrow path reads a 2M-row/10-col file in ~3.1 s vs ~10.5 s; production CSVs are wider (more columns survive until collate) so the absolute gap is larger. This was simply missed in the bf283fd collate pass.
findings.proposed_fix: from src.utils.csv_manager import fast_read_csv; df = fast_read_csv(df_path). No path normalization needed here (collate merges on the raw filepath strings it read itself, so self-consistent).
findings.expected_gain: ~3-4x on the stage's CSV load: ~7 s saved at 2M rows, ~35 s at 10M, more on wide frames. Small but free.
findings.risk: low — same already-accepted pipeline-wide tradeoff (pyarrow 1-ULP float parse, RFC-4180 quoting on read is moot); the env knob restores the old parser.

findings.title: to_webdataset sanitizes metadata per-cell in the worker hot loop (pd.isna + isinstance + hasattr per value per file)
findings.description: For every audio file, worker_fn loops over its ~20 metadata cells doing scalar `pd.isna(v)` (~1 us each — it is a dispatching pandas call), an isinstance float/nan re-check that pd.isna already covered, an isinstance Timestamp/Timedelta check, and `hasattr(v,'item')` to unbox numpy scalars. At 2M files that is ~40M pd.isna calls plus 80M+ auxiliary checks ≈ 1-2 minutes of pure Python in the default single-worker configuration — all of it recomputing the SAME answers because the types are fixed per column. Additionally line 91 stats every file (`audio_path.exists()`) immediately before `read_bytes()` — a redundant random-seek stat per file on the production HDD.
findings.proposed_fix: Sanitize once, vectorized, in load_metadata: convert datetime columns with .astype(str), numeric columns to native Python with df.astype(object).where(df.notna(), None) (NaN -> None, numpy scalars -> object), then to_dict('records') yields JSON-ready dicts and the worker body becomes `json_data = dict(meta)`. Drop the exists() pre-check and let the existing read_bytes try/except handle missing files (the exception path already `continue`s identically — note: today a missing file silently skips without counting an error, preserve that). Pin equivalence by comparing serialized json bytes for a fixture with NaN, int64, float, Timestamp, and string cells.
findings.expected_gain: ~1-2 minutes of worker CPU per 2M files removed, plus 2M random stat() calls on the HDD (seek-bound, likely another several minutes cold). Helps most at the config default num_workers=1 where this loop is the only CPU consumer besides tar writing.
findings.risk: medium — JSON bytes must stay identical (None vs NaN, int unboxing, Timestamp str format); needs the equivalence test above. The exists() removal is low risk (same control flow via the existing except branch).

findings.title: report.py missing-values scan: pure-Python csv.DictReader cell loop for CSV; parquet path decodes all columns when only string columns need scanning
findings.description: _read_csv_missing_summary iterates every row with csv.DictReader and runs `str(value).strip() == ""` per cell: at 2M rows x 30 columns that is 60M Python-level operations ≈ 2-4 minutes, single-threaded. It only runs when balalaika.parquet is absent (parquet is preferred at report.py:123-129), so it is the fallback path — but that is exactly the path taken when someone runs the report mid-pipeline before collate. The parquet path streams batches (good) but `iter_batches` with no column selection decodes EVERY column, while null counts for non-string columns are already available for free in row-group statistics (pq metadata `null_count`); only string columns genuinely need a data scan for the empty-string count.
findings.proposed_fix: CSV path: chunked `fast_read_csv`/pd.read_csv(chunksize=..., dtype=str, na_filter=...) with vectorized `s.isna() | s.str.strip().eq('')` per chunk — preserves the whitespace-only-counts-as-missing semantics exactly. Parquet path: pull null_count per column from `pq.ParquetFile.metadata` row-group statistics and pass `columns=[string cols]` to iter_batches so numeric/bool columns are never decoded.
findings.expected_gain: CSV fallback: minutes -> a few seconds at 2M rows. Parquet path: ~30-50% less data decoded (numeric columns skipped) — modest since report runs once per pipeline, ~10-30 s at production scale.
findings.risk: low — counts are provably identical (pin with a fixture containing whitespace-only cells, missing trailing cells, and nulls); report is a leaf stage with no downstream consumers of timing.

findings.title: recovery_from_meta decodes the entire episode into RAM before checking whether all output segments already exist (cold path)
findings.description: process_audio_file calls `AudioSegment.from_mp3(audio_path)` (line 28) — pydub decodes the WHOLE episode to raw PCM in RAM via an ffmpeg subprocess: a 1-hour 44.1 kHz stereo podcast is ~600 MB, and ThreadPoolExecutor runs num_workers of these concurrently (8 workers ≈ 5 GB, 32 ≈ OOM on 31 GB). The per-segment `os.path.exists(dest_path)` skip (line 43) happens AFTER the full decode, so on re-runs every already-completed episode is fully re-decoded for nothing (`saved` stays False, the source is kept, and the next run decodes it again — forever). Each kept segment then spawns another ffmpeg subprocess via segment.export (line 46). Bonus bug: recovery_from_meta_yamls.sh:22 invokes `taskset -с 0-24` with a CYRILLIC 'с' (U+0441), so taskset errors out and the launcher is broken as committed.
findings.proposed_fix: (1) Zero-risk: compute all dest_path names from episode_meta FIRST and return before any decode when every segment exists — identical outputs, kills the eternal re-decode on resume. (2) Optional: replace pydub with one `ffmpeg -ss start -t dur -i src out.mp3` per segment (input seeking, no full-episode RAM decode; ~0 MB resident vs ~600 MB) — but re-encode parameters must match pydub's defaults or output bytes change, so gate behind a knob. (3) Fix the Cyrillic 'c' in the launcher. This stage is not wired into base.sh (cold/manual path) — deprioritize beyond fix (1) and (3).
findings.expected_gain: (1) Resume runs skip completed episodes entirely: re-runs go from re-decoding the full corpus (hours, HDD-bound) to a metadata-only pass (seconds-minutes). (2) Peak RAM per worker ~600 MB -> ~tens of MB, enabling more workers on this node.
findings.risk: low for (1) and (3) — control-flow only, outputs identical; medium for (2) — produced mp3 bytes would differ unless encoder params are matched exactly.

areas.area_summary: Read all four files end-to-end plus the helpers they call (read_file_content in src/utils/utils.py, fast_read_csv/discover_audio_paths in src/utils/csv_manager.py, webdataset's TarWriter/ShardWriter source in the venv) and cross-checked against report.md §4.2/4.3 and .claude/analysis/denoising-export.json. The big prior wins (collate sidecar slab-scandir 17.6x, vectorized ASR consistency 1.9x, webdataset vectorized metadata load + sibling scandir) are genuinely in the code — but one claimed fix is NOT: report.md §8 says webdataset workers receive only their shard's metadata, while to_webdataset.py:212 still pickles the full dict to every worker (finding 2). The dominant remaining themes are peak-RAM blowups at production row counts (collate's triple-copy of all sidecar text, webdataset's N-way metadata duplication) rather than compute; no numba/C++/CUDA candidates exist here — the hot loops are allocation/syscall problems, not math. Audited per the leads and found NOT material: tar writing (ShardWriter writes plain uncompressed USTAR tars through default-buffered file objects, no fsync, sequential — fine for HDD); parquet write currently uses default snappy (a zstd knob folded into finding 1); shard worker balance is count-based not byte-based but moot at the config default num_workers=1 (noted in finding 2's fix). Trivia merged here: collate's dir_names_cache dict is shared across threads without a lock (benign last-writer-wins race, at worst a duplicate scandir); add_asr_consistency_column mutates the caller's frame via out = df (known, low); report.py's full-history table re-parses params JSON per row (negligible); recovery_from_meta launcher has a Cyrillic 'с' in `taskset -с` that breaks it outright (rolled into finding 6). recovery_from_meta is not wired into base.sh stages 12-14 and is treated as a cold path per instructions.

findings.title: base.sh auto-enables cudf.pandas, which silently disables the new pyarrow CSV fast paths and adds GPU-OOM risk on busy GPUs
locations: src/utils/csv_manager.py:196-222 (fast_read_csv routes to pd.read_csv low_memory=False when cudf proxy detected)
src/utils/csv_manager.py:297 (pyarrow writer also disabled under cudf)
src/utils/csv_manager.py:313-323 (chunked iloc.to_csv to an open file handle — guaranteed cudf->pandas fallback)
findings.description: base.sh probes `import cudf.pandas` and, if present, wraps EVERY stage in cudf.pandas.install() (opt-out only via BALALAIKA_DISABLE_CUDF=1). csv_manager deliberately detects the proxy and falls back off both pyarrow paths — so on any cudf-equipped node the measured 3.4x read / 4.1x write wins (report §4.1) are forfeited and replaced by cudf interception. Worse, the csv_manager workload is hostile to cudf.pandas: fcntl-locked read/merge/write cycles, set(...tolist()) (full D2H copy), .loc assignment from Python lists (_normalize_path_series:140), csv.DictWriter partials, chunked df.iloc[...].to_csv(f) to an open handle — each unsupported op triggers a whole-frame GPU->CPU fallback copy, and a multi-GB read_csv on a GPU already holding 11-14 GB of a training job risks OOM-ing the training process. On THIS node cudf is not installed (verified: import fails in .dev_venv) so nothing is measurable here — but on a RAPIDS-image production node this is a silent regression that can be slower than the pre-optimization baseline. The block's own comment claims it accelerates 'read_csv -> merge -> atomic write', which is exactly the path csv_manager now turns off under the proxy.
findings.proposed_fix: Flip the default to opt-in: only add the cudf prefix when BALALAIKA_ENABLE_CUDF=1 is set (keep the probe + message). Alternatively auto-disable when pyarrow is importable (it always is in this repo's lockfile) since the pyarrow engine already covers the CSV-heavy path without touching VRAM. Pure orchestration change, no Python code paths altered; outputs unchanged by construction (csv_manager already treats cudf as the byte-conservative slow path).
findings.expected_gain: On cudf-equipped nodes: restores the already-measured 3.4x/4.1x CSV read/write wins for every stage and removes a multi-GB VRAM allocation contending with training jobs. On nodes without cudf: zero change.
findings.risk: low — config/launcher default change only; outputs identical (cudf path was never byte-different, just slower/riskier). Keep the env knob so a node that genuinely benefits can opt back in.

findings.title: unprocessed_paths reads the full-width CSV (all ~17 columns) when it needs exactly 2
findings.description: Every stage calls unprocessed_paths once at startup; it does load_main_csv (full read of balalaika.csv: all columns, 3.06 s at 2 M rows per report §4.1), then a full-frame df.copy() inside _normalize_filepath_column, only to use two columns: 'filepath' and the stage's value column. At 2 M rows x 17 mostly-string columns the transient frame is multiple GB; at 10 M rows on the 31 GB node this is the single largest stage-startup allocation. The done-set construction itself is already vectorized (applied in the prior pass) — the remaining waste is purely read width. Cost: ~11 stages x one full-width read per run, each ~3-8x wider than needed.
findings.proposed_fix: Read with usecols=['filepath', column] through fast_read_csv. Since pyarrow (and the C fallback) raise on a missing usecols column, sniff the header first (read the first line with csv.reader — one syscall) and fall back to the current 'column missing => everything pending' branch without parsing the body at all (an extra win: today a missing column still pays the full read). Normalize only the 2-column frame (no full copy). Pending list is provably identical.
findings.expected_gain: ~2-4 s and ~70-90 % less transient RAM per stage at 2 M rows (linear in rows and width; ~x5 at 10 M). When the column doesn't exist yet (first run of a stage) the entire CSV parse is skipped.
findings.risk: low — same pending set; covered by the existing bench_csv_ops fixture and an equality assertion old-vs-new.

findings.title: _csv_duration_cache: slow C-engine read (callable usecols bypasses fast_read_csv) + 2M-iteration Python loop with per-row normalize and tqdm
locations: src/utils/audio_durations.py:57-62 (pd.read_csv with callable usecols — never uses the pyarrow engine)
src/utils/audio_durations.py:71-88 (per-row tqdm loop: normalize_path_string + set lookup + _positive_duration per row)
src/utils/audio_durations.py:37-49 (_normalise_requested_paths — second per-item Python pass over all requested paths)
findings.description: ensure_audio_durations runs at startup of music_detect, distillmos_process and antispoofing (3 stages/run). It reads balalaika.csv with pd.read_csv(usecols=<lambda>) — the lambda is unsupported by pyarrow, so this is the old single-threaded C engine, NOT the optimized fast_read_csv used everywhere else — then iterates all rows in Python under tqdm, calling normalize_path_string and a float() try/except per row. At 2 M rows that is roughly 4-6 s of C-engine parse plus 4-8 s of GIL-bound loop, i.e. ~10 s per stage x 3 stages, growing linearly (production datasets are larger). The whole thing computes: {normalized filepath -> positive total_duration} restricted to requested_norms.
findings.proposed_fix: (1) Read via fast_read_csv(target, usecols=['filepath','total_duration']) after a header sniff (same pattern as the unprocessed_paths fix). (2) Vectorize: s = _normalize_path_series(df['filepath']); d = pd.to_numeric(df['total_duration'], errors='coerce'); mask = d.gt(0) & s.isin(requested_norms); dict(zip(s[mask], d[mask])). Equivalence is a direct dict-equality test. _normalise_requested_paths can stay (it preserves (original, normalized) pairs).
findings.expected_gain: ~8-11 s -> ~1 s per duration-using stage at 2 M rows (x3 stages per pipeline run); removes a full-column Python object churn pass. Scales linearly with rows.
findings.risk: low — pure refactor of a pure function; pyarrow float parse can differ by 1 ULP from the C engine (already the documented, accepted tradeoff for every other read; durations are round(x,4) values where it is invisible). Keep BALALAIKA_CSV_ENGINE=c honoring via fast_read_csv.

findings.title: Periodic flush cycle is O(full CSV + all partials) per flush and re-runs drop_missing scandirs; mostly redundant for durability since partials are the actual recovery mechanism
locations: src/utils/csv_manager.py:616-647 (read_partial_csvs re-reads every partial in full each flush)
src/utils/csv_manager.py:877-902 (_count_partial_rows re-reads all partial bytes every 30 s poll)
src/utils/csv_manager.py:544-556 (drop_missing_files existence scan runs inside every periodic upsert for filter stages)
findings.description: Each periodic flush = full re-read of ALL partials (they only grow) + full main-CSV read + full-frame normalize/merge + full rewrite (+hardlink bak) — measured 14.73 s at 2 M rows (report §4.1). A stage that produces 2 M rows at flush_every_rows=100k performs ~20 flushes ≈ 5 minutes of pure state churn, O(N²/flush_rows) overall; for filter stages every flush additionally re-scandirs every dataset directory on the production HDD (drop_missing passes through at line 974). Crucially, the merger never deletes partials and every stage absorbs leftover partials at the next start (e.g. music_detect.py:279), so the periodic flush adds durability ONLY in the narrow case where a partial later becomes unreadable — otherwise it is pure freshness cosmetics for mid-stage observers. The poll loop also re-reads every partial byte-by-byte each 30 s to count newlines, even though partials are append-only. This is the remaining piece of report §9.5 (parquet/append-log state); the items here are cheaper increments that don't require the format decision.
findings.proposed_fix: Three independent, format-preserving steps: (a) make the per-flush partial reads and row counts incremental — cache (path -> byte offset, rows) and read/count only appended bytes up to the last complete newline (partials are append-only by construction of PartialCsvWriter); (b) skip drop_missing_files on periodic flushes (keep it for the final absorb only — mid-stage prunes are redone at the end anyway), removing the per-flush HDD directory sweep; (c) raise the default flush_every_rows (e.g. 1 M) or make the periodic flush time-only (flush_every_seconds), documenting that balalaika.csv may lag the partials mid-stage; the resume path is unaffected. (a) and (b) leave final outputs byte-identical; (c) changes only mid-stage CSV freshness.
findings.expected_gain: At 2 M rows: up to ~15 s saved per flush x ~20 flushes ≈ 4-5 min per row-producing stage; eliminates repeated full-partial reads (grows with stage progress) and the per-flush directory sweep on /mnt/hdd_6tb_1. Grows quadratically with dataset size under the current scheme.
findings.risk: low for (a)/(b) — end-of-stage CSV is provably identical (final absorb recomputes everything); medium-low for (c) — documented behavior knob (already exists in the csv: config block), no data-loss path since partials persist until absorbed.

findings.title: upsert_columns peak RAM ≈ 3-4 full frames: unconditional df.copy() in _normalize_filepath_column plus outer merge
locations: src/utils/csv_manager.py:487-542 (upsert: read -> normalize-copy -> outer merge -> per-column combine_first)
findings.description: Every upsert (stage-start absorb, every periodic flush, durations write-back, final absorb) holds: the freshly read frame (plus pyarrow's transient Table), a full deep copy from _normalize_filepath_column (the copy duplicates ALL columns even though only 'filepath' may change — and in steady state nothing changes because stored paths are already absolute and stripped), then the outer-merge result (another full frame with __incoming columns). On the 31 GB node this is the highest RAM watermark of every stage's bookkeeping; at 10 M rows x 17 columns it becomes an OOM candidate, especially while the training job also holds host RAM.
findings.proposed_fix: (1) In _normalize_filepath_column compute the normalized series first and only copy/assign when it differs: s = _normalize_path_series(df['filepath']); if s.equals(df['filepath']): return df — steady-state CSVs (absolute, pre-stripped paths) skip the whole duplicate. (2) For frames that are function-local (just read inside the same lock: load_main_csv, upsert at line 488-491, read_partial_csvs) assign the column in place instead of copying — they have no aliasing callers. (3) Optionally drop the boolean incoming_marker column trick in favor of an indicator merge ('_merge' from how='outer', indicator=True) to avoid one extra object column on the merged frame.
findings.expected_gain: Removes one full-frame deep copy per CSV touch (4-6 touches/stage): roughly 1-2 GB lower peak per merge at 2 M rows, several GB at 10 M; also saves the ~0.5-1 s the copy costs. Output frame identical.
findings.risk: medium — aliasing must be audited (callers of load_main_csv/ensure_main_csv that mutate the returned frame would now mutate shared state; a quick grep shows stages treat it read-only, but this needs the existing behavior tests + bench_csv_ops equality run). The s.equals() short-circuit alone is low-risk and captures most of the win.

findings.title: phonemizer/accents submit one ProcessPoolExecutor future per file — O(N) Future objects and IPC pickles in the parent at production scale
locations: src/utils/parallel.py:96-97 (one ex.submit per item, future_to_item dict holds all)
findings.description: run_per_gpu_pool submits every item up-front and keeps a future->item dict for the whole run. Each concurrent.futures.Future carries a threading.Condition + lock + callback list (~0.5-1 KB effective with dict overhead). For phonemizer/accents the items are individual file Paths: at a production backlog of 500 k-1 M pending sidecars the parent allocates ~0.5-1 GB just for futures/bookkeeping before any work happens, plus one pickle round-trip and executor scheduling (~tens of µs) per file. Punctuation already moved to slab submission for the 5x batching win — phonemizer and accents still submit per file even though FastG2P (report §4.9) batches internally per text and ruAccent processes one text per call.
findings.proposed_fix: Adopt the punctuation slab pattern: chunk pending_files into lists of e.g. 64-256 and make work_fn process a slab (a thin wrapper looping the existing per-file function, with the same per-file try/except so one bad file doesn't fail neighbors). Alternatively/additionally add a bounded submission window in run_per_gpu_pool (submit ~4x num_workers futures, refill on completion) so parent memory is O(workers) instead of O(items) for all callers without touching stage code.
findings.expected_gain: Parent RAM −0.5-1 GB at 500 k+ pending files; removes N pickle/scheduling round trips (~25-50 s at 1 M items, minor vs model time but free); makes the 31 GB node safe for large backlogs. Per-file outputs unchanged (sidecar writes are inside the worker).
findings.risk: low-medium — error accounting becomes per-slab; mitigate exactly as punctuation did (count actually-produced sidecars for stage status). Per-file fallback inside the slab keeps failure isolation.

findings.title: Duration probe workers spawn fresh interpreters that import the full torch/torchaudio stack just to read audio headers
locations: src/separation/music_detect.py:254 + distillmos_process.py:231 + antispoofing.py:316 (mp.set_start_method('spawn') — probe children re-import everything)
findings.description: The three duration-using stages set the global start method to 'spawn', so each of the (default 4) probe workers boots a fresh interpreter and safe_audio_duration's `import torchaudio` pulls in torch + CUDA libs: ~4-6 s startup and ~500-800 MB RSS per worker — ~2-3 GB transient RAM on the 31 GB node, concurrent with the parent that already has torch loaded. All of this to read a sample-rate/num-frames header. The cost hits any run where new files lack total_duration (first run after stage 1, or after re-chunking); steady-state reruns skip the pool entirely.
findings.proposed_fix: Probe with soundfile first for extensions libsndfile decodes exactly (.wav/.flac/.ogg/.opus — which is what the pipeline's chunked files are), keeping torchaudio as the fallback and as primary for .mp3 (ffmpeg-based info can differ from libsndfile on VBR mp3). soundfile imports in ~0.1 s at ~30 MB. Gate the reorder behind a small config/env knob if byte-equal reporting on mp3 sources must be guaranteed. Alternatively pass an initializer that pre-imports soundfile only, and never let torchaudio load in probe children.
findings.expected_gain: −2-3 GB transient RAM and ~−20 s pool startup per probing run (x3 stages when durations are cold); per-probe latency also drops (no torch dispatch).
findings.risk: medium if blanket-reordered (mp3 duration values could differ slightly between backends, changing length buckets); low if extension-gated as proposed — for wav/flac/ogg/opus both backends report frames/samplerate from the same header, value-identical (assertable on the 250-wav bench sample).

areas.area_summary: Read all 10 area files end-to-end plus base.sh and the consuming stages (punctuation/phonemizer/accents/music_detect/crest/antispoofing/distillmos) to verify what the prior pass already applied: pyarrow read/write, hardlink .bak, vectorized dedupe/done-set, scandir exist-masks, DirNameCache, single os.walk audio discovery, and the parallel.py arity/error-attribution bugs are all in place and were not re-reported. The biggest open item is the cudf.pandas auto-enable in base.sh, which on cudf-equipped nodes silently disables every pyarrow fast path and risks GPU OOM against training jobs (not measurable on this node — cudf is absent from .dev_venv). The remaining wins are: 2-column usecols reads for unprocessed_paths and the duration cache (the latter still uses the slow C engine via a callable usecols plus a 2M-iteration Python loop), incremental/leaner periodic flushes (the cycle is still O(full CSV + all partials) per flush and re-runs drop_missing scandirs, while partials — not the flush — are the actual crash-recovery mechanism), a full-frame deep copy removable from every merge, slab submission for phonemizer/accents (punctuation already does it), and soundfile-first duration probes to keep torch out of spawn'd probe workers. Checked and fine: work_shards (claim glob is one listing per claim at realistic shard counts ~10^3; bucketed prepare holds all paths in RAM ~100 MB/1M paths — acceptable), gpu.py, node_profile.py (profile cached), stage_status.py, audit.record_stage_summary (single-row append), PartialCsvWriter's flush-per-row (rows arrive at model speed, syscall cost is noise), and normalize_path_values' plain loop on the audio list (~1-2 s at 1M, already tqdm-free; vectorizable only by changing call signatures — not worth the churn).

Read all 6 stage files end-to-end plus their helpers (src/utils/datasets/separation.py, src/utils/audio_durations.py, src/utils/work_shards.py, csv_manager PartialCsvWriter, audit.safe_audio_duration) and microbenchmarked the per-call primitives (Path.resolve 37.9us, torchaudio.info 2.43ms, soundfile.info 88us; bench audio is 16kHz mono PCM_16; numba 0.65 present; distillmos importable; musicdetection package+weights ABSENT so stage 4 is analysis-only end-to-end). The prior pass already fixed the GPU-side basics (pinned staging + real non_blocking for distillmos/music_detect loaders, TRT fp16 for Spectra-0 4.25x, single-CSV-read distillmos_filter parent), so the remaining waste is almost all CPU/metadata work that idles the GPUs: a redundant 2.4ms-per-file torchaudio.info pass per DistillMOS shard, per-shard full-CSV reloads in music_detect, syscall-heavy Path.resolve in every per-file loop, full-clip decodes for a 4s antispoofing crop, and dead-data duration probes in the filters. Minor items not promoted to findings: claim_work_shard does sorted(glob) per claim = O(shards^2) scans (work_shards.py:227, small at current shard counts); antispoofing_filter parses the main CSV twice via scored_rows (antispoofing_filter.py:294,313 — second load skippable when absorbed==0); PartialCsvWriter flushes every row (csv_manager.py:770) and distillmos/music_detect emit 2+ eagerly-formatted loguru debug f-strings + perf_counter pairs per FILE even at INFO level (distillmos_process.py:116-145, music_detect.py:182-195) — together tens of seconds per million rows, trivially batchable with a flush-every-K knob and a level guard.

findings.title: DistillMOS workers re-probe audio lengths per shard (sort_by_length) on shards that are already duration-sorted; shared JSON cache is clobbered across workers so it never hits
findings.description: Every claimed shard builds a dataloader via create_distillmos_dataloader, which calls sort_by_length -> estimate_audio_lengths: a SINGLE-THREADED torchaudio.info loop inside the GPU worker process, measured at 2.43 ms/file warm-cache (ffmpeg StreamReader spin-up per call). But the shards were already written duration-sorted by prepare_length_bucketed_work_shards (work_shards.py:204-206) using durations main() computed at distillmos_process.py:286 — the probe re-derives information the stage already has. The JSON cache (podcasts_path/distillmos_sorted_files.json) is shared across workers/shards: each worker's shard set is disjoint from the cached list, so it takes the 'cache stale, rebuilding' branch (separation.py:103), re-probes, and overwrites the file — with 2 GPU workers it is also a write race. Cost: ~24 s of GPU-idle serial CPU per 10k shard; at 2M files / 200 shards that is roughly 80 minutes per run, worse on the HDD where each info() is a random read.
findings.proposed_fix: Feed shard files to DistillMOSDataset in their on-disk order (add an assume_sorted=True flag to create_distillmos_dataloader or simply bypass sort_by_length from this stage) and delete the racy JSON cache path. No numba/C++ needed — it is pure dead work.
findings.expected_gain: Removes ~2.4 ms/file of serial CPU inside GPU workers: ~20-30 s per 10k shard, ~80 min per 2M-file stage run; GPU stops idling between shards.
findings.risk: low — shard order is preserved verbatim, so batch composition only changes where sort_by_length ordered duration-ties differently than the shard sort; verify MOS scores on the 250-wav bench sample (expected identical / sub-noise).

findings.title: music_detect re-reads (and may rewrite) the entire balalaika.csv once per shard via ensure_audio_durations inside the worker loop
findings.description: _process_files calls ensure_audio_durations for EVERY claimed shard. Each call runs _csv_duration_cache: a full pd.read_csv of balalaika.csv (plain pandas with a usecols lambda, not the pyarrow fast path) plus a per-row tqdm Python loop calling normalize_path_string over every CSV row, and then upsert_columns -> a full atomic CSV rewrite whenever any duration was probed. At 2M rows that is ~8-12 s per call x N shards (200 shards at shard_size 10k ~= 30+ min) plus rewrite churn that races with the PeriodicCsvMerger. distillmos and antispoofing already hoist this to main() (distillmos_process.py:286, antispoofing.py:352) — music_detect was missed. Two compounding issues: (a) music_detect uses plain prepare_work_shards (line 314) instead of length-bucketed shards, so LengthBasedBatchSampler batches inside random 10k shards still mix 1s..15s clips and WavLM pads to batch max; (b) the lookup audio_lengths.get(str(path), 0.0) at line 163 misses whenever predict_proba returns a path form differing from the dict keys, silently triggering a 2.4 ms safe_audio_duration probe per file.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist ensure_audio_durations to main() over all pending paths (mirroring distillmos_process.py:286), switch to prepare_length_bucketed_work_shards, and pass each shard its own duration slice; normalize the lookup key the same way the dict keys were built.
findings.expected_gain: Removes (N_shards-1) full CSV reads + rewrites: ~30 min at 2M rows / 200 shards, scaling linearly with both; plus 10-30% fewer padded WavLM frames from bucketed shards.
findings.risk: low — duration values and scored outputs unchanged; the CSV/duration helper path is benchmarkable on this node without the model (music_detection.safetensors absent, so end-to-end stage timing is not).

findings.title: _csv_duration_cache: per-row Python normalize + tqdm over the whole CSV with the slow pandas parser (runs at start of distillmos, antispoofing, and per-shard in music_detect)
findings.description: The duration cache load does pd.read_csv(target, usecols=lambda col: ..., low_memory=False) — a Python-callable usecols forces per-column callback and the C engine, not the pyarrow path that gave 3.4x in the CSV layer — followed by a pure-Python for loop with tqdm over all rows calling normalize_path_string per row and a membership check against requested_norms. At 2M rows this is ~4-8 s of parse plus ~4-6 s of Python loop, paid at every distillmos/antispoofing stage start (and per shard in music_detect until finding #2 is fixed).
findings.proposed_fix: Read with engine='pyarrow' and explicit usecols=['filepath','total_duration']; vectorize the normalization (paths are absolute -> pure string ops; reuse csv_manager.normalize_path_values or a vectorized .str pipeline), filter via .isin(requested_norms), then dict(zip(...)). No numba needed — pandas vectorization suffices.
findings.expected_gain: ~3-5x on this step: ~10 s -> ~2-3 s per stage start at 2M rows; multiplies with finding #2 for music_detect.
findings.risk: low — same dict contents; pyarrow 1-ULP float caveat is irrelevant for round(x,4) durations; existing csv fixtures can pin equality.

findings.title: Antispoofing dataset decodes and preemphasizes the full clip, then keeps only a 4.04 s random window — ~3x wasted decode I/O per long clip, now the likely bottleneck after the TRT 4.25x win
findings.description: AntiSpoofingDataset.__getitem__ does a full torchcodec decode of the clip (pipeline chunks are up to 15 s = ~480 KB of 16 kHz PCM_16; resample no-ops since audio is already 16 kHz), converts to float32, runs preemphasis over the FULL waveform, then _pad_random crops 64600 samples (4.04 s). For any clip longer than 4.04 s, 60-73% of the decoded bytes and the preemphasis work are discarded. With TRT fp16 the GPU side is down to ~6.6 ms/item (52.7 ms / batch of 8), so 4 loader workers doing ~3-6 ms full decodes plus float conversion are at or past parity — on the production HDD, where each file read is ~3.7x more bytes than needed, the loader becomes the hard bottleneck. The existing dataloader_wait / dataloader_audio_load debug perf lines can quantify this directly.
findings.proposed_fix: Probe frames from the wav header (soundfile, 88 us), draw the random start, then seek+read exactly 64601 samples (one extra leading sample so preemphasis y[n]=x[n]-a*x[n-1] reproduces the full-decode value at the window's first sample bit-exactly for PCM wav); keep the current full-decode path for files shorter than num_samples (the repeat branch) and for non-wav containers. Gate with a config knob (e.g. antispoofing.ranged_decode).
findings.expected_gain: ~2-3.5x less loader I/O and CPU per >4 s item; on HDD reads ~130 KB instead of up to 480 KB per file. Stage wall gain depends on how loader-bound it is post-TRT — measurable here on the 250-wav bench.
findings.risk: medium — decode backend changes (torchcodec -> soundfile) for the cropped read; bit-exact for PCM wav and the stage is already non-deterministic by design (random crop, documented in report section 6), but should be knob-gated and validated on bench wavs.

findings.title: DistillMOS inference is plain fp32 torch; its fixed 122880-sample internal windows make it the same shape-static TRT-fp16 candidate that gave Spectra-0 4.25x
findings.description: run_inference_worker loads distillmos.ConvTransformerSQAModel and runs it in fp32 (only TF32 matmul from apply_torch_perf_defaults). The model internally pads/segments every input into fixed 122880-sample windows concatenated along the batch axis, so the real compute shape is (total_windows, 122880) — static width, exactly the property that made Spectra-0 a clean TensorRT candidate (224 -> 52.7 ms/batch, 4.25x). Two escalation levels: (a) cheap: torch.autocast('cuda', dtype=torch.float16) around sqa_model(batch), config-gated; (b) full: one-time ONNX export with a dynamic window-batch dim, run via ORT TRT fp16 reusing the pinned-profile session pattern already written for antispoofing (create_session, antispoofing.py:119-139) so trailing partial batches don't trigger rebuilds. fp16 also halves activation VRAM — valuable while both GPUs carry the training job.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a config-gated fp16/TRT path: start with autocast (no new artifacts), then ONNX+TRT-fp16 with engine cache under runtime.trt_cache_path; default off until score divergence is measured and documented like the Spectra-0 entry.
findings.expected_gain: Est. 1.5-3x inference throughput on an idle GPU plus ~2x activation-VRAM cut; on this contended node only relative numbers (warmup saw saturation at 57.8-60.5 it/s with the training job co-resident).
findings.risk: medium and outputs change: MOS shifts of order 0.001-0.01 expected (Spectra-0 fp16 logit delta was 0.0085); files near the distillmos_filter threshold can flip, so this must stay behind a config knob with a measured divergence bound vs fp32 on the 250-wav bench.

findings.title: distillmos_filter deletion phase iterates and writes a partial row for EVERY scored file instead of just deletion candidates
findings.description: run_deletion_workers builds items for ALL scored paths (mos not NaN), not just mos<threshold. Each worker then pays per row: resolve_path (37.9 us measured Path.resolve), a writer.write with per-row flush, and a processed_counter bump — for the typically 95%+ of files that are merely kept (deleted=False). The resulting N-row partial is then re-parsed and outer-merged into the main CSV (lines 437-443) although it adds no values (preserve_existing=True, scores already present). The sibling antispoofing_filter already does this right: it shards only deletion_candidates (antispoofing_filter.py:321) and derives the audit from the baseline frame. At 2M scored rows the kept-file churn is ~2M x ~55 us ~= 2 min of worker time, a 2M-row partial write+parse+merge (~15-30 s), and worker already_done sets holding millions of strings.
findings.proposed_fix: Shard only candidates (mos < threshold); compute audit files_in/hours_in from the already-loaded df (the exact numbers print_preview computes at line 383) instead of audit_from_filter_partials row counts; keep partial rows only for actually-attempted deletions.
findings.expected_gain: Deletion phase drops from O(all scored) to O(candidates): ~10-100x fewer partial rows, minutes saved at 2M rows plus a large RAM cut in workers and the absorb step.
findings.risk: medium — filter_summary.csv files_in/hours_in must be reproduced from the dataframe (pin with an equality test mirroring tests/test_distillmos_filter.py); resume semantics for kept files change (today they are needlessly re-written each run).

findings.title: Duration probes call torchaudio.info first (2.43 ms) where soundfile (88 us, 27x cheaper) is exact for the pipeline's wav files; antispoofing_filter probes every deletion candidate only to write a never-read column
findings.description: safe_audio_duration tries torchaudio.info first — measured 2.43 ms/call warm-cache (it spins up an ffmpeg StreamReader per call) — with soundfile as fallback, although soundfile.info is 88 us and returns identical frames/samplerate from the same header for the PCM wav files every pipeline stage produces. This multiplies across the ensure_audio_durations probe pool (audio_durations.py:95-118, chunked over a ProcessPool) and all per-file fallbacks. Worst case: antispoofing_filter.run_worker calls safe_audio_duration on EVERY deletion candidate before unlinking it (line 200) and writes duration_s to the partial — but the final audit recomputes all hours from baseline['total_duration'] (lines 386-395) and absorb runs with value_columns=[] (lines 364-369), so the probed value is consumed by nothing. 100k deletions = ~4 min of probe CPU plus 100k random header reads on the HDD, entirely for dead data.
findings.proposed_fix: (a) Reorder safe_audio_duration: soundfile first, torchaudio fallback for exotic containers. (b) In antispoofing_filter, delete the probe and carry total_duration from the candidates frame into the shard (or drop the duration_s field — it is provably unread).
findings.expected_gain: 27x cheaper duration probes pipeline-wide for wav; antispoofing_filter deletion phase ~2-5x faster wall on probe-bound runs and far fewer HDD seeks.
findings.risk: low — identical values for wav/flac/ogg (verified probe parity is testable on the 250 bench wavs); torchaudio fallback retained; removing the dead column is output-neutral by construction.

findings.title: resolve_path (Path.resolve, 37.9 us + per-component syscalls) runs twice per file in every separation inner loop, on paths that were already normalized — and is inconsistent with how already_done sets are keyed
findings.description: Each stage resolves every file path twice (pending-filter pass + post-inference write pass) via resolve_path = str(Path(p).resolve()), measured at 37.9 us/call (readlink/lstat per path component). The shard files were already written from unprocessed_paths output, which is normalized by the string-ops normalize_path_values — csv_manager.py:87-99 explicitly documents that Path.resolve is 'prohibitively expensive when repeated over tens of millions of rows' and that absolute paths are trusted verbatim. So this is ~76 us of redundant syscall work per file: ~2.5 min per 2M files per stage, across 5 stage files. There is also a latent correctness hazard: PartialCsvWriter.already_done builds its set with normalize_path_values (csv_manager.py:786-790, verbatim absolute strings) while the loops probe membership with resolve_path — on a symlinked dataset root the strings differ, so every done-check would silently miss and files would be re-scored on resume.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace resolve_path with normalize_path_string in the per-file loops (or normalize once when reading the shard). Pure string op for absolute paths, no vectorization framework needed.
findings.expected_gain: ~2-3 min saved per stage at 2M files (more on HDD where lstat seeks) and removal of the symlink resume-mismatch hazard.
findings.risk: low — byte-identical output strings for absolute real paths (the only kind the shards contain); on symlink/.. inputs the new behavior matches the convention the done-sets and CSV layer already use.

areas.area_summary: Checked all six files end-to-end (preprocess.py 1022 lines, preprocess_existing_chunks.py 626, sortformer_onnx.py 442, smart_turn/inference.py 85, offline_svad.py 162, utils/datasets/preprocess.py 204) plus helpers in csv_manager.py, gpu.py, audio_postprocessing.py and configs/config.yaml. The CSV/state layer this stage uses was already optimized by the prior pass; the model-adjacent hot paths flagged in report.md §9.2 are all still unapplied and verified present, and I add four new findings: a hardcoded TRT-fp16 provider for the dynamic-shape streaming Sortformer (perf + silent output divergence), a per-chunk double/triple audio decode in the ACTIVE existing_chunks+fuse config, an O(all-chunk-rows) RAM accumulation/pickle in raw mode, and decode fully serialized with GPU inference (loader workers=0). Trivia merged here rather than as findings: src/libs/smart_turn/inference.py is imported nowhere yet builds an ORT session + WhisperFeatureExtractor at import time (delete or guard, preprocess.py uses offline_svad.SmartVAD instead); pending_metadata_paths re-runs .map(normalize_path_string) over the done rows even though load_main_csv already vectorized-normalizes the filepath column (csv_manager.py:370-376), ~2-4 s redundant at 2M rows, and _metadata_complete_mask does astype(str).str.strip() per object column (~1 s/column at 2M rows) once per run (preprocess_existing_chunks.py:95-101,120); raw mode splits files round-robin by count not duration with no work-stealing (preprocess.py:896-898 — existing_chunks mode already uses claimable work shards, raw mode could reuse them); torch.cuda.empty_cache() runs after every source file (preprocess.py:702-704 — throttle to every N files, but keep occasional calls since the GPU is shared with training); offline_svad.py:142's explicit torch.cuda.synchronize is redundant because output[0,0].item() already blocks. Sortformer and SmartTurn ONNX files are absent on this node, so model-touching findings are marked benchmarkable=false; the pure-CPU numpy post-processing and the decode-redundancy findings are benchmarkable with synthetic tensors / the 250-wav bench sample.

findings.title: existing_chunks fuse mode decodes every chunk file twice (and raw fuse mode decodes short files twice on top of the loader decode)
findings.description: This is the ACTIVE production path (config: input_mode=existing_chunks, fuse_audio_preprocessing=true, data on /mnt/hdd_6tb_1). Per chunk file: (1) DiarizationDataset.__getitem__ opens AudioDecoder(path, sample_rate=16000) and fully decodes for diarization; (2) _postprocess_existing_chunk immediately re-opens the SAME file via torchaudio.load_with_torchcodec at native rate for crest+LUFS; (3) kept files are rewritten. With loader workers=0 the second read usually hits the page cache (reads are ~1 s apart), so the real waste is the second full ffmpeg decode (~10-30 ms CPU per 15 s chunk), which at 500k+ production chunks is hours of single-thread CPU that is *serialized with GPU inference* in the current config — and it becomes real double disk I/O once loader prefetch is enabled or memory pressure evicts the cache. Note _postprocess_existing_chunk does not depend on the diarization result at all, only on the audio. Raw mode has the same pattern for files <= limit_dur: the loader already decoded the file at 16k, then preprocess.py:598-600 re-opens and get_all_samples() the whole native file.
findings.proposed_fix: Read the file bytes once in the dataset worker and construct both AudioDecoder instances from the in-memory bytes (torchcodec accepts bytes sources): return (path, bytes_or_native_tensor, audio_16k, sr). Decoding twice from identical bytes keeps outputs byte-identical (same decoder, same input) while removing the second file open/read and moving both decodes into loader workers where they overlap GPU diarization (see the loader-workers finding). Optionally add a config knob to derive the 16k tensor by resampling the native decode (single decode) — that diverges slightly (torchaudio resample vs ffmpeg swresample) so it must be gated and is not the default. Chunks are <=15 s (~0.5-6 MB native), so carrying both tensors through the batch is fine on 31 GB.
findings.expected_gain: Removes ~50% of decode CPU in the active stage path; combined with loader-worker overlap, est. 1.3-2x stage throughput in existing_chunks+fuse mode at production scale; removes one file-open+read per chunk on the HDD (matters when cache is cold).
findings.risk: low for the bytes-once variant (decodes are bit-identical; verifiable on the 250-wav bench sample without any model); medium for the single-decode-resample knob (diarization input changes slightly — gate it).

findings.title: Diarization source decode fully serialized with GPU inference (loader workers=0, prefetch_factor=0, missing worker_init_fn)
findings.description: diarization_loader_workers defaults to 0 in config, so every torchcodec decode+resample (both modes) runs synchronously in the GPU process between Sortformer calls — the GPU idles for the full decode time of every file, and in existing_chunks mode also for the native re-decode and the LUFS rewrite. The DataLoader already supports workers/prefetch (datasets/preprocess.py:200-203). Two blockers for just flipping the knob: (a) diarization_prefetch_factor is 0 and torch requires prefetch_factor >= 1 when num_workers > 0, so setting workers>0 with the current config crashes; (b) create_diarization_dataloader sets no worker_init_fn, unlike the crest loader (datasets/preprocess.py:51 pins torch.set_num_threads(1)), so each decode worker can claim all 48 threads.
findings.proposed_fix: Default diarization_loader_workers to 2 and diarization_prefetch_factor to 2 (or make prefetch_factor floor at 1 when workers>0), and add a worker_init_fn pinning torch.set_num_threads(1) to create_diarization_dataloader. RAM caution for raw mode long-form audio: a 2 h source decoded to 16 k mono fp32 is ~460 MB, so workers*prefetch must stay small (2x2 worst case ~1.8 GB buffered) — document this; existing_chunks chunks are ~1 MB each so it is free there.
findings.expected_gain: Decode time hidden behind GPU inference: est. 20-40% wall-clock on decode-bound raw files, more in existing_chunks mode where per-file CPU work (2 decodes + crest + pyloudnorm) is comparable to the GPU diarize time of a 15 s chunk.
findings.risk: low — ordering preserved (shuffle=False), decode results identical; only buffering RAM changes. End-to-end gain not measurable on this node (Sortformer ONNX absent); loader-only throughput is.

findings.title: Hardcoded TensorRT-first provider with global fp16 for the dynamic-shape streaming Sortformer session
findings.description: init_models builds Sortformer providers with get_onnx_providers(gpu_id, use_tensorrt=True, ...) — hardcoded, unlike every other stage which gates TRT behind a config key — and runtime trt_fp16 defaults to True. The streaming session's inputs have genuinely dynamic shapes across calls (spkcache lengths cycle through ~{0,124,188,248,312}, fifo {0,124}; sortformer_onnx.py:197-208), and no TRT profile min/max shapes are pinned (the spectra-0 work in report.md pinned profiles; this session does not). Consequences: several multi-minute engine builds on first run (one per shape signature), possible heavy graph partitioning of the streaming model into many small TRT subgraphs (often slower than CUDA EP), a 4 GiB TRT workspace competing with the user's training job on the shared 16 GB GPU, and — most importantly — fp16 silently changes every diarization prediction, hence all segment boundaries and chunk metadata, with no documented fidelity check (unlike spectra-0's measured 0.0085 logit delta). Telling detail: sortformer_onnx.py's own default provider list has the TRT block commented out (lines 70-78), suggesting it was tried and disabled, but init_models overrides it with TRT-first anyway.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a preprocess.use_tensorrt config knob (mirroring separation/antispoofing) defaulting to False so Sortformer runs on CUDAExecutionProvider until TRT is actually measured on a node with the model; if TRT is kept, pin trt_profile_min/max/opt_shapes for the small closed set of shape signatures and decide fp16 explicitly per-model rather than inheriting the global trt_fp16 flag.
findings.expected_gain: Avoids multi-minute first-run engine builds and potential steady-state regression from subgraph partitioning; frees the 4 GiB TRT workspace on the shared GPU; removes an undocumented output divergence (fp16) from stage 1, which feeds every downstream stage.
findings.risk: low to apply (CUDA EP is the conservative choice and matches the module's own default); flag: current TRT-fp16 behavior means today's outputs already differ from fp32 — switching to CUDA EP changes outputs vs the current default, so document the flip.

findings.title: SmartVAD runs one feature-extraction + ONNX call per segment in raw mode (batchable to one call per file slab)
findings.description: apply_eos_classification (raw mode only; not called in existing_chunks) loops every timeline segment and calls smart_vad.predict_endpoint one at a time. Each call: np.pad to exactly 8 s, a WhisperFeatureExtractor invocation computing a full 800-frame mel on GPU (H2D of 128000 floats + kernels), one batch-1 ONNX run via IOBinding, a redundant full torch.cuda.synchronize (line 142 — output[0,0].item() already blocks), and ~2-5 ms of Python/FE overhead. A 2 h source with ~4 s turns produces ~1800 segments, i.e. ~1800 sequential round trips ≈ 20-30 s of VAD per file, all GPU-underutilized. The model's output is already shaped (B, 1) (offline_svad.py:127-130), so the graph supports batching; all inputs are padded to the identical fixed (80, 800) shape, so stacking is trivial; and the downstream merge loop (preprocess.py:261-289) consumes predictions in order with no cross-segment dependency, so batched results are semantically identical.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a predict_endpoint_batch(list_of_segments) to SmartVAD: pad/truncate all segments (the existing _truncate_audio), pass the list to one feature_extractor call (transformers FE accepts batches), one io-bound session.run on (N, 80, 800), threshold the (N,1) output; call it from apply_eos_classification in slabs of a config-capped smart_vad_batch_size (16-32; (32,80,800) fp32 is ~8 MB, safe on the shared GPU). Drop the explicit synchronize.
findings.expected_gain: Est. 3-5x on the VAD step of long multi-turn files (kernel-launch/FE/Python overhead amortized); VAD is one of the three serial per-file costs in raw mode, so several seconds saved per long source file.
findings.risk: medium — batched cuBLAS/cuDNN kernels can differ from batch-1 by float-epsilon, and probabilities sit against a 0.4 threshold, so knife-edge segments could flip; gate behind a knob (default on) and document, mirroring the RUPunct batching proof done in §4.4. Model file absent on this node, so equivalence must be proven where smart-turn-v3.2-gpu.onnx exists.

findings.title: Sortformer streaming loop bounces all state GPU->CPU->GPU every ~10 s chunk (numpy session.run + full-window mel D2H)
findings.description: extract_mel_features computes the spectrogram on GPU then copies the whole window to host as numpy (line 133: ~46 MB D2H per 900 s window). _streaming_update then feeds every chunk as numpy dicts to session.run — ORT re-uploads chunk features (~512 KB), spkcache (~385 KB) and fifo (~254 KB) H2D and copies preds + new_embs (~260 KB) D2H on every one of the ~91 calls per 900 s window, plus a fresh output allocation per call. All FIFO/spkcache state lives in CPU numpy (with per-chunk np.concatenate reallocations at lines 223-243) purely so Python can slice it, then goes straight back to the GPU.
findings.proposed_fix: Use ORT IOBinding with torch CUDA tensors via OrtValue.from_dlpack — the repo already has the exact working pattern in offline_svad.py:124-143 including the CPU fallback. Keep the mel features tensor resident on GPU (slices of a contiguous (1,T,128) tensor along dim 1 are contiguous, so per-chunk views bind directly), keep spkcache/fifo as torch CUDA tensors updated with torch.cat/narrow, and only pull chunk_preds (a few KB) to host per chunk for _binarize. The spkcache compression math (_compress_spkcache) can stay on CPU initially by syncing only when compression triggers (~once per chunk in steady state but operating on ~640 KB), or be ported to torch later.
findings.expected_gain: Eliminates ~1.4 MB of per-chunk H2D/D2H plus the 46 MB-per-window mel copy; prior analysis estimated 10-25% of Sortformer inference wall time, biggest on short chunks (existing_chunks mode) where per-call overhead dominates the 124-frame compute.
findings.risk: medium — IOBinding itself is numerically a no-op (same tensors, no recompute), but the refactor touches the streaming-state bookkeeping; needs the absent ONNX model to validate bit-equality of preds. Keep the numpy path as fallback for CPU-EP nodes, as offline_svad does.

findings.title: Pure-Python per-frame loops in Sortformer CPU post-processing (_binarize, silence profile, spkcache top-k/gather) + string format/parse round trip
findings.description: _binarize iterates Python-level over every (frame, speaker) scalar — 45k iterations (~5-15 ms) per 900 s window, 752 per 15 s chunk — then formats segments into 'start end speaker_N' strings that preprocess.py:127-144 immediately splits and re-floats. _update_silence_profile recomputes a running mean one 512-dim frame at a time (~124 Python iterations per popped chunk, every ~10 s of audio). _get_topk_indices builds Python lists from a full argsort and _gather_spkcache assigns 188 rows one by one on every compression trigger (~once per 10 s on long files); _boost_topk_scores full-argsorts where argpartition suffices. Aggregate: low milliseconds per 10 s of audio of GIL-bound work inside the single per-GPU process — small per file but multiplied by hundreds of thousands of files, and it runs interleaved with (not overlapped by) GPU calls.
findings.proposed_fix: Plain numpy, no numba needed (though numba 0.65.1 is installed if wanted): _binarize via boolean threshold + np.flatnonzero(np.diff) edge detection per speaker column, returning (start, end, spk) tuples directly and deleting the string round trip (parse_diarization_output keeps its tuple branch, preprocess.py:140-141, so callers need no change); _update_silence_profile as the closed-form weighted mean (mean*n + sil_embs.sum(0))/(n+k); _get_topk_indices/_gather_spkcache via np.argpartition + vectorized fancy-index gather with a mean_sil_emb fill mask.
findings.expected_gain: Est. 1-5% of stage CPU time (more on long-form raw audio); removes O(frames x speakers) Python work from the hot per-file path. Small but cheap and self-contained.
findings.risk: low for _binarize/top-k/gather (integer/threshold logic, bit-identical outputs, provable on synthetic preds arrays on this node — no model needed); the silence-profile closed form changes float summation order (~1e-7 relative) — either keep a sequential-equivalent np.add.reduce ordering or document the epsilon.

findings.title: Raw mode accumulates every chunk-row dict in worker RAM and pickles the full list back, only to compute a count and an hours sum
findings.description: _run_diarization_shard appends every chunk metadata dict to a results list (line 793) even though each row was already streamed to the partial CSV one line earlier; the full list is returned through the ProcessPoolExecutor future (pickled across the process boundary, line 816) and the parent accumulates all shards into all_results (line 935). The ONLY uses are len(all_results) for files_out and a sum of total_duration for hours_out (lines 961-967). At production scale (millions of chunks) a ~10-field dict costs ~600-1000 B, so this is multiple GB held in the child, a comparable pickle buffer spike at shard end, and another copy in the parent — on a 31 GB node shared with training, this is the stage's largest avoidable RAM consumer. existing_chunks mode already avoids this with mp.Value counters.
findings.proposed_fix: Return only aggregates from the shard: {'rows': n, 'duration_s': total, 'crest_audit': ...} — or compute files_out/hours_out from the DataFrame that absorb_partial_csvs already returns (preprocess.py:949 currently discards it; preprocess_existing_chunks.py:568-576 already uses exactly this pattern). Drop results.append and all_results entirely.
findings.expected_gain: O(total_chunks) RAM -> O(1): several GB saved at production scale in both child and parent, plus eliminates a multi-hundred-MB pickle at shard completion; no throughput change at small scale.
findings.risk: low — audit summary numbers computed from the same rows; only caveat is rows absorbed at startup from a prior interrupted run must not be double-counted (the startup absorb at lines 878-888 already separates them).

findings.title: Stage startup does 3 full CSV reads + 1 unconditional full CSV rewrite + 3 path-normalization passes (both stages)
findings.description: Both stages run the same scaffold: ensure_main_csv() reads balalaika.csv (read 1); absorb_partial_csvs() is called with bootstrap_audio_paths=audio_paths, and because bootstrap is not None the early-return at csv_manager.py:681-682 never fires — upsert_columns ALWAYS runs even with zero leftover partials, re-reading the full CSV (read 2), re-normalizing every audio path a 2nd time (csv_manager.py:494-499), concat+drop_duplicates over N_csv+N_audio rows, and doing a full atomic write (+.bak hardlink) of the entire CSV even when nothing changed; then unprocessed_paths() re-reads the full CSV a 3rd time (csv_manager.py:573) and re-normalizes the audio paths a 3rd time (csv_manager.py:575-577, they were already resolved by discover_audio_paths). At 2M rows with the already-optimized pyarrow layer (report §4.1: load 3.06s, write 3.63s) that is roughly 2 redundant reads (~6s) + 1 redundant write (~3.6s) + 2 redundant normalize passes (~2-4s) ≈ 10-14s of pure waste per stage start, ×2 stages in this area (the pattern repeats in other stages too). Scales linearly with rows; the unconditional rewrite also churns the HDD and the .bak hardlink every run even when the stage is a steady-state no-op under fuse_audio_preprocessing: true.
findings.proposed_fix: Reuse one loaded DataFrame across the three steps at the call sites: have ensure_main_csv return its df (it already does), make absorb_partial_csvs accept/return it, and only call upsert_columns when (partials non-empty) OR (set(audio_resolved) - set(df.filepath) is non-empty); add an unprocessed_paths(df=...) overload that takes the in-memory frame and pre-resolved paths. Safe here because PeriodicCsvMerger has not started yet and stages run sequentially. No numba/C++ needed — this is pure call-graph plumbing.
findings.expected_gain: ~10-14s per stage start at 2M rows (×2 stages), linear in dataset size (~×5 at 10M rows); removes one full-CSV write per stage start — the biggest remaining HDD-write waste in these stages' steady-state no-op runs.
findings.risk: low-medium. balalaika.csv values are identical; skipping the no-change rewrite trivially preserves bytes (today the file is rewritten with identical content). Must keep the new-file registration semantics (new files on disk still get rows) — pin with a test that adds a file between runs.

findings.title: Crest stage decodes the entire dataset on a single thread (config 1 worker / batch 1 / 0 loader workers)
findings.description: config.yaml pins num_workers_crest_factor: 1, crest_factor_batch_size: 1, crest_factor_loader_workers: 0 (set 2026-05-26/31, untouched by the perf pass; the code defaults at crest_factor_remover.py:278-279 are 256/2). With loader_workers=0 the DataLoader decodes in-process with zero overlap, so one thread does a full torchcodec decode of every pending file. CrestFactorDataset.__getitem__ already reduces each file to 4 scalars (peak, sum_squares, length, sr) inside the loader worker, so the stage is embarrassingly parallel with near-zero RAM per in-flight item; crest_factor_worker_init already pins torch to 1 thread per loader worker. At production scale (hundreds of thousands of chunks, up to 900s each per config chunk_duration: 900) a single-threaded decode pass is tens of hours; the per-batch compute (calculate_crest_factors_from_stats) is microseconds. Note: under the default fuse_audio_preprocessing: true this stage is normally a catch-up no-op, so the win applies to non-fused configs and backfill runs.
findings.proposed_fix: Config-only change: raise crest_factor_loader_workers to 8-16 and crest_factor_batch_size to 64-256 (and/or wire batch_size: auto). Keep num_workers_crest_factor=1 so partial-CSV row order is exactly preserved (torch DataLoader with shuffle=False returns items in dataset order regardless of worker count). No code change required.
findings.expected_gain: Near-linear with loader workers until HDD/CPU saturates: roughly 6-12x on the stage's decode-bound wall clock; tens of hours → hours on a full production backfill. Invisible at 250-file bench steady state but directly measurable there with the existing perf dataloader_wait DEBUG lines.
findings.risk: low, outputs provably unchanged: per-file peak/sum_squares are computed per item in the dataset, batch ops are elementwise, rounding to 4 decimals identical at any batch size; with num_workers=1 the partial CSV is byte-identical.

findings.title: Loudness stage runs decode → LUFS measure → re-encode strictly serially in one process (config 1/1/0)
findings.description: loudness_num_workers: 1, loudness_batch_size: 1, loudness_loader_workers: 0 means each file is fully decoded (torchcodec, C), then LUFS-measured (pyloudnorm, GIL-bound Python+numpy), then re-encoded and written back (torchcodec, blocking) — three phases that never overlap, on one core, while 47 others idle. Per-file split is roughly decode ~30-40%, measure ~5-15%, encode ~45-60%, so simple prefetch already hides a third of the time. Caveat for RAM (the 31GB node): LoudnessNormalizeDataset returns the FULL native-rate waveform through collate; a 900s 48kHz stereo float32 item is ~345MB, and with loader workers each in-flight item transits torch shared memory — batch_size × prefetch_factor × workers items can be resident, with no per-item size guard.
findings.proposed_fix: Config: loudness_loader_workers: 2-4 with loudness_batch_size kept at 1-4 and prefetch_factor 1-2 (bounds in-flight RAM to ~0.7-2.8GB worst case); optionally loudness_num_workers: 2-4 to parallelize the GIL-bound measure+encode across processes (HDD-permitting). Code option for a further win without RAM growth: move the synchronous _write_audio call onto a 1-thread writer executor inside the worker so file N's encode overlaps file N+1's measure (torchcodec encode releases the GIL); add a config knob defaulting to off.
findings.expected_gain: 1.4-1.8x from decode prefetch alone; 2-4x combined with 2-4 worker processes until the HDD's mixed read/write seeks saturate. Like crest, full value shows on non-fused configs / backfill runs (fused stage 1 normally leaves this stage a no-op).
findings.risk: low for loader workers with num_workers=1: identical floats reach process_audio_file (shm round trip preserves bits), same encode, same row order — md5-identical outputs, verifiable against the report §5 bar on the 250-wav bench. num_workers>1 keeps audio bytes identical but can reorder rows across partials (values identical after keyed upsert) — flag/gate that separately. Writer-thread variant is medium risk (error-path ordering) and should stay behind a knob.

findings.title: pyloudnorm BS.1770 gating is a per-block Python loop with 4x redundant squaring — vendor a bit-exact vectorized meter (hot in BOTH the fused stage-1 path and this stage)
findings.description: normalize_audio_loudness calls pyloudnorm Meter.integrated_loudness, which (a) loops in Python over channels × numBlocks computing np.sum(np.square(data[l:u])) per 400ms block — with 75% overlap every sample is squared ~4x and a 19200-element temp is allocated per block (a 900s 48kHz chunk = ~9000 blocks, ~173M redundant element ops); (b) builds the blockwise-loudness list with a per-block Python list comprehension calling np.sum on a tiny list + scalar np.log10 (meter.py:89); (c) does the two gating passes with nested list comprehensions (meter.py:93-107). For a 60s mono 48k chunk the gating Python part is ~15ms of a ~50ms measure (the rest is the 2 scipy.lfilter K-weighting passes, already C). This runs per chunk in the FUSED stage-1 path (postprocess_audio_tensor at preprocess.py:402 / preprocess_existing_chunks.py:242) — i.e. on every chunk at production scale even with the default config — plus per file in this standalone stage.
findings.proposed_fix: Vendor a meter in audio_postprocessing.py with two tiers. Tier A (default, provably bit-exact): precompute sq = np.square(filtered) once per channel and keep the per-block np.sum over slices of it (identical pairwise summation over identical floats → identical bits), and vectorize (b)/(c) with the same ufuncs elementwise (np.log10 on an array is per-element identical to scalar calls; np.mean over fancy-indexed blocks matches np.mean over the equivalent list). Tier B (knob-gated, e.g. loudness_fast_meter): fully vectorize block sums via the 4-non-overlapping-subblock decomposition (step = block/4) or reduceat — last-ULP LUFS may differ, so verify md5 on the 250-wav bench before enabling. numba is installed (0.65.1) but is the wrong tool here: its sequential accumulation cannot reproduce numpy's pairwise float32 sums bit-for-bit, breaking the byte-identical-output bar.
findings.expected_gain: Tier A: measure 1.3-1.6x; Tier B: up to ~2.5x (lfilter then dominates). Stage-level ~5-15% on standalone loudness; a larger relative share of the fused stage-1 postprocess CPU (no decode in that path). At millions of chunks this is tens of CPU-hours, and it also removes ~9000 temp allocations per long chunk.
findings.risk: medium overall, but Tier A is designed bit-exact (same gain → same written samples; verify md5-identical on the 250-file bench, same bar as report §5). Tier B must stay behind a config knob with documented divergence. Vendoring also fixes the latent non-thread-safe _METER_CACHE (Meter mutates self.blockwise_loudness).

findings.title: Normalized audio is rewritten in place non-atomically — a crash mid-encode permanently destroys the source file
findings.description: _write_audio (and the fused save at preprocess_existing_chunks.py:265) calls torchaudio.save_with_torchcodec directly on the original path. SIGKILL/OOM/power-loss mid-encode leaves a truncated file; because the loudness_normalized marker is only written after a successful save, resume re-decodes the now-corrupt file, counts an error, and the original audio is gone — there is no backup. This is the same class of bug the perf pass already fixed for _rover_phonemes.txt sidecars (report §4.9). Not a speed win, but it gates how aggressively the parallelism findings above can be deployed (more in-flight files = more exposure).
findings.proposed_fix: Write to <path>.tmp<pid> in the same directory and os.replace() onto the target (same-directory rename, no extra HDD seeks; identical bytes land at the final path). Optionally fsync before rename behind a knob for the production HDD.
findings.expected_gain: No throughput change (rename is free); eliminates a permanent-data-loss window per written file, which matters at hundreds of thousands of writes per run.
findings.risk: low — final bytes identical (verify md5 on the 250-wav bench); only failure-path behavior changes (leftover .tmp files need a startup sweep or unique names).

areas.area_summary: Read all three files end-to-end plus their helpers (src/utils/datasets/preprocess.py, csv_manager.py call paths, work_shards usage) and the installed pyloudnorm source; cross-checked against report.md §4-6 and the two prior analysis JSONs. Already done and NOT re-reported: pyarrow CSV layer, .bak hardlink, bounded crest audit fallback (now sample-capped at crest_factor_remover.py:437-448), loudness skipped-counter fix, the documented peak-is-ignored and lossy-re-encode known issues, and the md5-identical 250-file validation. The area's model math is trivial (crest is 4 scalars/file); the remaining waste is (1) startup CSV redundancy — an unconditional full-CSV rewrite plus two redundant full reads per stage start caused by bootstrap_audio_paths defeating absorb's early return, (2) zero parallelism in the shipped config (1 process / 0 loader workers / batch 1 for both stages — the code defaults are saner than config.yaml), (3) the pyloudnorm per-block Python gating loop, which is the one real numba/vectorization candidate and is hot even under the default fused path via postprocess_audio_tensor, and (4) a non-atomic in-place audio rewrite that risks data loss. Trivia not worth separate findings: calculate_crest_factors at crest_factor_remover.py:71-77 is dead code (only the _from_stats variant is called); the prior analysis's 'duplicate decode' in calculate_crest_factor is overstated (the second .to(float32,cpu) is a no-op returning self once converted, only the multichannel mean allocates); unprocessed_paths re-normalizes already-resolved paths (folded into finding 1); mp.Value counter increments are non-atomic (reporting only); per-row PartialCsvWriter flush is negligible vs decode/encode; no resampling happens in these stages (lead's concern — confirmed absent). All five findings are benchmarkable on this node: CPU-only work, 250 real wavs present, and the stages already emit perf DEBUG lines (dataloader_wait, process_file, audio_save) that attribute decode/measure/encode time per file.

findings.title: Accents stage is completely unbatched: 3-6 batch-1 ONNX calls per sentence, one file per future (the RUPunct situation before §4.4, times four models)
locations: src/accents/accents.py:70-77 (run_per_gpu_pool over individual files, no slabs)
.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/yo_homograph_model.py:100-110 (predict_yo_homographs: batch-1 session.run per sentence whenever the sentence contains 'е', i.e. almost always)
.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/omograph_model.py:74-90 (odd-hypothesis path: one session.run PER HYPOTHESIS at batch 1)
findings.description: RUAccent.process_all splits each file into sentences and, per sentence, runs: 1 stress-usage BERT (120 MB ONNX) at batch 1, 1 yo-homograph BERT (14.5 MB) at batch 1 if the sentence contains the letter 'е' (virtually every Russian sentence), an omograph NLI model (368 MB) — batched within the sentence only on the even-hypothesis path, otherwise one session.run per hypothesis — and one accent-model run per unknown stressed word. The stage submits one future per FILE, so there is zero cross-file or cross-sentence batching: every call is a latency-bound batch-1 GPU round trip with Python tokenize/softmax/aggregate between calls. This is exactly the shape RUPunct had before the §4.4 fix (measured there: 48.8 -> 252 texts/s at batch 64, 5.2x). With ~2 sentences/file that is ~4-6 ONNX calls per file; at a plausible ~10-20 files/s/GPU, 1 M production files cost ~14-28 GPU-hours per run.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the punctuation fix plus the fast_g2p pattern: (1) feed the stage slabs of files (reuse the punctuation slab code); (2) a thin 'fast_ruaccent' wrapper (keep weights/tokenizers/dicts from the installed package) that collects all sentences in a slab, dedupes them, and runs stress-usage and yo-homograph as padded batches (both are plain token-classification BERTs — same mechanics as the already-batched RUPunct); (3) batch omograph NLI pairs across sentences; (4) batch accent-model words. No numba/C++ needed — this is pure GPU batching. Numerics caveat: the wrapper must reproduce ruaccent's exact quirks to stay char-identical, notably OmographModel.classify's softmax over the WHOLE batch (omograph_model.py:91-100, e_x/e_x.sum() with no axis) and AccentModel's 0.55 score threshold; prove equivalence with the same char-identical harness used for §4.4 (60+ varied texts, batched vs per-file) and gate with a config knob defaulting to the proven path.
findings.expected_gain: 3-8x stage throughput (RUPunct analog measured 5.2x at batch 64); ~10-25 GPU-hours saved per 1 M-file production run; also cuts per-call H2D/D2H round trips
findings.risk: medium — padded-batch matmuls can flip argmax/threshold decisions on knife-edge scores (same class of risk as §4.4 and §4.9, both of which proved out char-identical / documented); requires equivalence proof + knob

findings.title: RUAccent loads a rule engine + koziev POS-tagger/lemmatizer (~200 MB of assets) per worker that NO inference path ever uses
