locations: .dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/ruaccent.py:83-88 (downloads koziev/rulemma + rupostagger + 168.8 MB ruword2tags.db per fresh node)

.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/rule_accent_engine.py:19-27 (loads accents.json, forms.json, Lemmatizer (rulemma.dat 16.7 MB), RuPosTagger (2.4 MB CRF + 9.7 MB ruword2tags.dat + sqlite db))
src/accents/accents.py:26-31 (load(..., tiny_mode=False) triggers it in every worker)
findings.description: grep over the whole installed package shows self.rule_accent is assigned in load() and never referenced again — _process_accent uses only the accents dict and accent_model. Yet every accents worker (one per GPU here, N on bigger nodes) downloads (first run: ~198 MB — ruword2tags.db 168.8 MB + rulemma.dat 16.7 MB + ruword2tags.dat 9.7 MB + CRF model 2.4 MB, written INTO site-packages) and then parses/loads the lemmatizer dictionaries and tagger at init. That is pure dead weight: seconds of init and an estimated 100-500 MB resident RAM per worker on a 31 GB node, plus a wasted ~200 MB per-node download.
findings.proposed_fix: Stub the load before calling accentizer.load() in init_process — e.g. monkeypatch ruaccent.rule_accent_engine.RuleEngine.load to a no-op (and skip the koziev download branch), or carry a 2-line fork patch. Provably output-identical: the attribute is never read (verifiable by grep + the existing stage tests). Keep it behind a config knob if paranoid, but there is no code path that can diverge.
findings.expected_gain: Per worker: seconds off init + est. 100-500 MB RAM (measure exact RSS delta on first load); one-time ~200 MB download avoided per fresh node; biggest relative win when num_workers > 1 on the 31 GB box
findings.risk: low — dead code elimination; outputs byte-identical

findings.title: Accents workers create 4 ONNX sessions with default thread pools: ~4 x physical-core intra-op threads, spin-wait enabled, on a box shared with the training job
locations: src/accents/accents.py:21-31 (load API passes providers only; no way to pass sess_options)
findings.description: Each of the 4 InferenceSessions defaults intra_op_num_threads to all physical cores (~24 here) with allow_spinning on — ~96 spin-capable threads per worker, ~192 across the two per-GPU workers, on the 48-thread box that also runs the user's training dataloaders. With CUDA EP most matmul lands on GPU, but pre/post CPU nodes and the spin-wait between the stage's MANY small batch-1 runs (see finding 1) burn cores and cause scheduler thrash. Session creation also redundantly graph-optimizes the 368 MB omograph model per worker.
findings.proposed_fix: In init_process, before accentizer.load(), wrap onnxruntime.InferenceSession (functools.partial-style monkeypatch or tiny fork patch) to inject SessionOptions with intra_op_num_threads=2-4, inter_op=1, and add_session_config_entry('session.intra_op.allow_spinning','0'). Optionally expose as accent.ort_intra_op_threads in config. Outputs from CUDA-EP runs are unaffected by CPU pool sizing; for the rare CPU-fallback nodes results are deterministic per ORT op partitioning at fixed thread count (pin the chosen count).
findings.expected_gain: Removes ~100+ idle/spinning threads per worker; 1.5-3x for the stage when num_workers>1 or when co-running with training (current measured contention pattern); near-zero cost otherwise
findings.risk: low — CUDA EP outputs unchanged; document thread count for CPU-EP nodes

findings.title: No word-level memo for AccentModel.put_accent — same out-of-dictionary word re-runs ONNX in every new sentence (plus get_outputs() rebuilt per call)
locations: .dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/ruaccent.py:204-220 (_process_accent calls put_accent for every dict-miss multi-vowel word, per sentence)
.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/accent_model.py:33-50 (put_accent: slow CharTokenizer + batch-1 session.run + session.get_outputs() name map rebuilt on EVERY call)
.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ruaccent/ruaccent.py:246 (only cache is sentence-level lru_cache(4096) — whole-sentence repeats only)
findings.description: put_accent(word) is a pure function of word.lower() (deterministic ONNX run), but results are never cached at word granularity — only identical whole sentences hit the lru_cache. ASR transcripts repeat OOV words (names, slang, ASR misspellings) heavily under Zipf; every occurrence in a distinct sentence pays a CharTokenizer pass (pure-Python per-char slow tokenizer), a batch-1 session.run, and a rebuilt output-name dict. At production scale (millions of files x ~1-3 OOV words each) this is millions of avoidable ONNX calls. Same applies in milder form to the yo/omograph dictionary lookups (already dict-based, fine).
findings.proposed_fix: Add a word -> stressed-word dict memo in the stage worker (wrap accentizer.accent_model.put_accent with an unbounded-or-LRU cache keyed on word.lower()), cache the get_outputs() name map at load, and optionally persist the memo across runs/workers exactly like the phonemizer oov_cache (§4.9 pattern: fingerprint-keyed pickle + flock merge). Outputs identical by construction (same session, same input bytes). Combine with finding 1's batched wrapper: dedupe unique miss-words per slab and run them as one padded batch.
findings.expected_gain: 2-10x fewer accent-model calls depending on word repeat rate; with persistence, re-runs skip nearly all of them; also removes per-call Python tokenizer cost
findings.risk: low — memoization of a pure function; persistent-cache variant inherits the proven §4.9 locking design

findings.title: Punctuation 'produced' accounting re-stats every pending file one-by-one at stage end
findings.description: After the pool finishes, main() walks ALL pending files and calls Path.exists() per file to count produced sidecars — O(N) serial stat() calls. On the production HDD with hundreds of thousands to millions of pending sidecars, and dentry caches partially evicted after hours of stage runtime, this is the exact per-file stat storm that §4.7 removed from the pending scans (DirNameCache exists precisely for this). Cost: minutes of random HDD I/O for a log-only counter.
findings.proposed_fix: Reuse src/utils/sidecars.DirNameCache: one scandir per directory, then count name membership — identical result set (the equivalence was already verified for the pending-scan use of DirNameCache on a 50 k fixture). 5-line change.
findings.expected_gain: O(#files) stats -> O(#dirs) scandirs at stage end; minutes saved on cold-cache HDD at production scale; zero behavior change
findings.risk: low — same counting semantics; symlink handling already covered by DirNameCache

findings.title: Punctuation slabs pad every text to the slab's longest text — sort slab by text length before the batched pipeline call
locations: src/punctuation/punctuation.py:121-124 (slabs built in CSV discovery order)
src/punctuation/punctuation.py:83 (model([texts], batch_size=len(pending)) — one padded forward per slab)
findings.description: Slabs are cut from pending_files in discovery order, so each 64-text batch pads to its longest member; BERT attention is O(L^2), so one long transcript in a slab of short utterances multiplies the whole batch's FLOPs. The worker already reads all slab texts into memory (lines 66-77) before calling the pipeline, so length information is free at that point. With ASR-segment length distributions (heavy short tail, occasional long segments) the padding waste is real but bounded.
findings.proposed_fix: Inside make_punct_batch, sort the in-memory (path, text) list by len(text) before the pipeline call and pass a sub-batch size (e.g. keep pipeline batch_size at the profile value but hand workers larger slabs, say 8x batch_size, so sorting actually groups similar lengths) — write-back order is irrelevant since each output goes to its own file. Per-text outputs are independent of batch composition in exact math; the §4.4 char-identical proof should be re-run once with sorted batches to pin fp-level equivalence (same harness, 60 texts).
findings.expected_gain: 1.2-1.8x on the punctuation forward pass for skewed length distributions; nothing on uniform lengths
findings.risk: low-medium — same fp-batch-composition risk class already accepted and proof-tooled in §4.4; rerun the proof

findings.title: RUPunct has no effective max_length: a >512-token text makes the whole slab fail, re-runs all its files one-by-one, and can poison the worker's CUDA context
locations: src/punctuation/punctuation.py:82-97 (slab-level except: whole slab retried per file = 2x model work for the good files)

.dev_venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/transformers/pipelines/token_classification.py:254 (truncation = True only honors model_max_length — which is the 1e30 sentinel for RUPunct, verified on this node)
findings.description: Verified: RUPunct/RUPunct_big's tokenizer has model_max_length = the VERY_LARGE_INTEGER sentinel while the model's max_position_embeddings is 512. The pipeline's truncation flag is therefore a no-op, so any transcript tokenizing past 512 feeds out-of-range position ids into nn.Embedding — on CUDA that is a device-side assert, which (a) fails the whole 64-file slab, (b) triggers the per-file fallback that re-runs the 63 good files (2x work), and (c) a device assert typically poisons the worker's CUDA context so every subsequent slab in that worker errors until the pool dies. Rover texts are per-VAD-segment and usually short, so this is rare — but one long segment in a production run can silently turn a worker into an error generator for the rest of the stage.
findings.proposed_fix: Set tokenizer.model_max_length = 512 at init and pass stride (e.g. 128) to the pipeline ctor — the TokenClassificationPipeline natively chunks + aggregates with a fast tokenizer and aggregation_strategy='first' (token_classification.py:195-215). Texts under 512 tokens produce byte-identical output (chunking only engages past the limit); over-limit texts change from 'hard-fail + worker poisoning' to 'fully punctuated', a strict improvement worth documenting rather than gating.
findings.expected_gain: Removes the 2x slab re-run and worker-poisoning failure mode; throughput-neutral on the common path; long files go from lost to processed
findings.risk: low — short-text outputs provably unchanged; long-text outputs change from error to result (document in report)

areas.area_summary: Punctuation (stage 8) is in good shape after the §4.4 batching: slab-batched pipeline calls, profile-driven batch size, DirNameCache pending scans, per-file fallback. Residuals found there are the stage-end per-file stat recount, in-slab padding waste, and a verified no-op truncation config that turns one >512-token text into a slab failure with CUDA-context-poisoning potential. Accents (stage 9) is the material target: the installed ruaccent runs every sentence through 3-6 batch-1 ONNX calls file-at-a-time (the exact pre-§4.4 RUPunct shape, so the 5.2x batching result is a grounded estimate), loads a never-referenced rule engine (~200 MB of koziev assets, downloads into site-packages) in every worker, creates 4 sessions with default ~24-thread spinning pools on a box shared with training, and has no word-level memo for the pure-function accent model. All accents findings are benchmarkable on this node after a one-time ~530 MB HF asset download (ruaccent imports fine from .dev_venv; assets not yet downloaded locally). Minor items not promoted to findings: accents process_file swallows all exceptions so write_stage_status always reports processed=len(pending) (observability bug); punctuation/accents Path.write_text is non-atomic while phonemizer got the tmp+rename fix in §4.9 (truncated sidecars survive resume); punctuation's code default num_workers=4 vs config's 1 would cost 4x1.4 GB VRAM per GPU with little gain now that batching saturates one worker; optional fp16 RUPunct (1.4 GB fp32 BertForTokenClassification) could give ~1.5-2x + half VRAM but would not be char-identical, so only behind a knob.

findings.title: Phonemizer submits one ProcessPool future per file — dispatch overhead + multi-GB parent RAM at production scale
findings.description: run_per_gpu_pool eagerly calls ex.submit() once per pending file and builds future_to_item for ALL items before the as_completed loop starts (parallel.py:96-97). Measured on this node: ~0.26 ms per no-op task round-trip (0.06 ms submit + 0.2 ms drain) through a single executor manager thread. Warm-path phonemizer work is only ~3-6 ms/file after the §4.9 optimizations (report: ~2 ms g2p for a fully-cached text + read + atomic write), so IPC/bookkeeping is ~5-13% of per-file wall and the manager thread caps aggregate throughput near ~4-5k files/s regardless of worker count. Worse for RAM: at 2 M pending sidecars the parent materializes 2 M Future + _WorkItem + pickled-Path entries (~0.5-1 KB each) ≈ 1-2 GB plus the as_completed waiter set, on a 31 GB node — and the submit loop alone takes ~2 min before tqdm shows progress. Punctuation (stage 8) already solved exactly this with slab submission; phonemizer was left per-file.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror punctuation: slice pending_files into slabs of ~128-512 paths (plain strings, not Path objects), submit one future per slab, and have process_text's wrapper loop the slab catching per-file exceptions and returning a per-file error list so accounting stays per-file (punctuation switched to counting produced sidecars — same pattern works here). No numba/C++ needed; this is pure orchestration. Optionally stream slabs with a bounded in-flight window instead of submitting all upfront to flatten the RAM curve completely.
findings.expected_gain: ~1.1-1.3x warm-path stage throughput (removes ~0.26 ms/file IPC and manager-thread serialization), minus ~1-2 GB parent RSS and ~2 min of silent submit time at 2 M pending files; gains scale linearly with file count.
findings.risk: low — file outputs byte-identical; only error-reporting granularity changes (per-slab futures with per-file errors collected inside the worker), same divergence punctuation already accepted.

findings.title: Denoising saves files synchronously inside the GPU inference loop (HDD write + re-encode blocks next batch)
findings.description: _process_files runs session.run, then in the same thread loops the batch items doing torchaudio.save_with_torchcodec to the production HDD (in-place over the source file) plus a partial-CSV row per item, before pulling the next batch. The dataloader overlaps decode, but nothing overlaps encode+write: ffmpeg open/encode/close + HDD write is ~20-200 ms per file (more for opus/flac re-encode of 10-20 s @48 kHz), i.e. plausibly 10-30% of loop wall against a heavy MossFormer2 batch (~0.5-2 s for batch_size=2). The GPU sits idle for the whole save loop. The prior analysis listed this; report.md applied only the throwaway-session fix (§4.6), so this is still open in current code.
findings.proposed_fix: Hand (path, enhanced int16/float array, length) to a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor (2-4 threads; ffmpeg encode releases the GIL) and perform writer.write + already_done.add + counter updates in the completion callback, with a small queue cap (e.g. 8 items) so RAM stays bounded (~8 × 3.7 MB). Keep per-file try/except so error attribution is unchanged, and drain the pool before mark_work_shard_done so a shard is only 'done' after all its saves landed.
findings.expected_gain: ~1.1-1.4x stage wall-clock on the HDD dataset (hides essentially all save latency behind inference); bigger when use_tensorrt makes inference faster and save becomes a larger fraction.
findings.risk: medium — written bytes per file identical, but completion ordering of partial-CSV rows changes and shard-done semantics must wait on the writer pool; MossFormer2 ONNX absent on this node so end-to-end gain can't be measured here (save-path cost alone can be timed).

findings.title: Denoising dataloader rebuilds the torchaudio resample kernel for every file
findings.description: DenoisingDataset.__getitem__ calls torchaudio.functional.resample per file. The functional API recomputes the sinc kernel on every call; torchaudio's own docs state transforms.Resample 'precomputes and reuses the resampling kernel'. Verified in the installed torchaudio source on this node. Source material is not 48 kHz (the model rate), so effectively every production file pays a kernel build (~1-5 ms for awkward ratios like 44.1k→48k where gcd=300 gives a 147→160 polyphase kernel) on top of the conv. At hundreds of thousands of files that is tens of CPU-minutes burned inside loader workers, which directly throttles GPU feed rate when decode is the bottleneck.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep a module-level (or dataset-instance) dict {orig_freq: torchaudio.transforms.Resample(orig_freq, 48000)} and reuse it; same kernel parameters → bit-identical output tensors. One-line vectorization-style fix, no numba needed.
findings.expected_gain: ~1-5 ms saved per resampled file; ~10-40 CPU-minutes per 500 k files inside loader workers, and correspondingly higher loader throughput when decode-bound (typical with batch_size=2 and 4 workers).
findings.risk: low — outputs bit-identical (same kernel math, just cached); measurable loader-only on the 250-wav bench sample with a forced source rate.

findings.title: Denoising loader workers run with unlimited torch intra-op threads — CPU oversubscription against ORT and the second GPU worker
findings.description: create_denoising_dataloader sets no worker_init_fn, so each spawned loader worker inherits torch's default intra-op thread count (up to all 48 threads on this node). With 2 GPU workers × num_workers=4 (config) that is up to 8 decode processes each spinning a full OMP team for resample conv1d and channel mean — potentially hundreds of runnable threads contending with the two ORT sessions (4 intra + 4 inter threads each) and tqdm/CSV threads. The crest/preprocess dataset already fixed this exact problem with torch.set_num_threads(1) in its worker init (preprocess.py:52); denoising never got the same treatment.
findings.proposed_fix: Add worker_init_fn=lambda _: torch.set_num_threads(1) (mirroring crest_factor_worker_init) to the denoising DataLoader. Per-file work is already parallelized across the 4 workers, so per-op threading only adds scheduler thrash.
findings.expected_gain: Typically 1.1-1.5x loader throughput and much lower context-switch load on a 48-thread box also running the user's training dataloaders; zero VRAM/RAM cost.
findings.risk: low — conv1d parallelizes over output positions (per-output reduction order unchanged), so resampled tensors should stay bit-identical; cheap to verify by diffing loader outputs at 1 vs default threads on the bench sample.

findings.title: TensorRT optimization profile for denoising is tuned to 1-second inputs while real batches are 10-20 seconds
findings.description: add_denoising_trt_profile_options sets trt_profile_opt_shapes to {batch}x1x48000 (exactly 1 s at 48 kHz) with max {batch}x1x960000 (20 s). Length-bucketed shards mean real padded lengths cluster at the duration buckets, typically 5-15 s (240k-720k samples) — 5-15x the opt point. TRT selects kernel tactics at the opt shape, so the engine actually used for nearly every batch runs with tactics tuned for sequences an order of magnitude shorter; for attention/FFT-heavy MossFormer2 blocks that commonly costs real throughput. Also trt_timing_cache_enable is set without a trt_timing_cache_path, and trt_detailed_build_log=True spams logs on every engine build.
findings.proposed_fix: Make the opt shape configurable (denoising.trt_opt_seconds, default ≈ the dominant bucket, e.g. 720000 = 15 s) and pass trt_timing_cache_path next to the engine cache. One-time engine rebuild per node (cached thereafter).
findings.expected_gain: Unknown without the model — plausibly 1.2-2x TRT-path throughput on long buckets based on typical tactic-mismatch penalties; zero change when use_tensorrt=False.
findings.risk: medium — outputs can shift within fp16 tactic tolerance (the path is already fp16 via runtime.trt_fp16), and the engine rebuilds once; gate behind the new config knob and validate against CUDA-EP outputs when the ONNX file is available.

findings.title: A single >20 s file kills a denoising GPU worker mid-shard (collate raises through the DataLoader, shard abandoned)
findings.description: denoising_collate raises RuntimeError when padded length exceeds max_padded_len=960000 (20 s @48 kHz). That exception propagates out of the DataLoader iterator, escapes _process_files (no try around the loop body for iterator errors), kills run_worker, and the worker process exits — its claimed shard stays .running.N (never done) and the worker claims nothing further for the rest of the run. With the default 15 s overflow bucket (len_ge_015s) holding ALL >15 s files, one 21 s chunk dooms the entire overflow shard (up to 10 k files of wasted/lost work) and halves GPU capacity for the remainder of the run. main() already has every file's duration in hand at sharding time (denoising.py:443) but never uses it to enforce the model's hard input cap. Related edge: batches whose padded length is below the TRT min profile (8000 samples, i.e. all-sub-0.17 s batches padded to 384) would similarly fail the profile check.
findings.proposed_fix: At shard-prep time, split paths with duration > MODEL_MAX_PADDED_LEN/48000 (minus a safety margin for resample rounding) into a logged skip list written to stage status (or route them to a future chunked overlap-add path); optionally also wrap the dataloader iteration so an unexpected iterator error fails the shard, marks it, and lets the worker continue with the next shard.
findings.expected_gain: Avoids losing a GPU worker plus an entire 10 k-file shard per oversized file — on a multi-day production run this is the difference between a logged skip and a silent 2x slowdown/abort.
findings.risk: low — pure-CPU repro (no model needed); behavior change is crash → explicit documented skip for files the model cannot accept anyway; processed-file outputs unchanged.

areas.area_summary: Phonemizer (stage 10) is in good shape after the §4.9 FastG2P rewrite — the only material residual is the orchestration around it: run_per_gpu_pool still submits one future per file (punctuation already moved to slabs), costing ~0.26 ms/file IPC and 1-2 GB of parent futures-RAM at 2 M pending. Denoising (stage 11, analysis-only since the MossFormer2 ONNX is absent here) has four open items: the synchronous in-loop save, per-file resample-kernel rebuild, uncapped torch threads in loader workers, and a TRT opt-profile tuned to 1 s inputs; plus a robustness/throughput trap where any >20 s file kills a worker and abandons its shard. Checked-and-fine: pending_sidecar_chain/DirNameCache scans (already scandir-cached), the ONNX graph-metadata input-name read (§4.6 applied), int16 IPC between loader and worker (halves shared-memory bytes, model expects int16-range floats), pin_memory=False (correct for ORT/numpy), PartialCsvWriter per-row flush (buffered, no fsync — cheap), and work-shard claim/glob costs (trivial at ~200 shards/2 M files). Trivia worth recording: process_text re-stats the already-pending output (phonemizer.py:35, one extra HDD stat/file, kept as a cheap resume guard); each phonemizer worker holds the 93 MB G2P dict (measured) plus a rules memo capped ~100 MB — fine at the configured 1 worker/GPU but multiplies if num_workers is raised (a marisa-trie or shared-memory dict would cut it ~5-10x); denoising noise padding uses unseeded torch.randn so outputs are already nondeterministic run-to-run and batch-composition-dependent (pre-existing, worth documenting before any 'outputs unchanged' claim for this stage); torchaudio.save_with_torchcodec overwrites the SOURCE audio in place non-atomically — a kill mid-save permanently corrupts the input with no partial row recording it (pre-existing data-loss risk, fix is tmp+rename like the phonemizer sidecar); mp.Value counters use non-atomic += across processes (status-accounting only).

findings.title: Spawned workers ignore the configured log level: per-row/per-batch DEBUG lines emit to stderr in production hot loops
findings.description: setup_logging() (INFO sinks, per runtime.log_level) is only called in each stage's main(). Every heavy stage then uses mp spawn (set_start_method('spawn') in 11 stage files) for its per-GPU workers / ProcessPoolExecutor initializers, and none of the worker entrypoints (run_inference_worker, init_process, worker_init_fn) reconfigure loguru. A spawned child freshly imports loguru, which installs its DEFAULT stderr sink at level DEBUG — verified empirically on this node: a spawn child emits logger.debug() even when the parent configured INFO-only sinks. DataLoader workers fork from those spawned workers and inherit the same DEBUG sink. Consequence: the per-batch perf lines (distillmos_process.py:116,125), the PER-ROW partial_write line (distillmos_process.py:142), and the PER-FILE dataloader_audio_load lines in dataset __getitem__ (datasets/separation.py:36/50/187/194, datasets/preprocess.py:37-47; similar in denoising) all emit unconditionally in production. At 2M files that is ~4-6M loguru record builds + stderr writes per GPU stage (~20-50us each, the per-row one serialized with the inference loop) ~= 2-5 minutes of pure logging per stage, interleaved with tqdm bars; and because the default sink is stderr-only, none of it reaches the rotating file sink, so the DEBUG perf data is also lost when you actually want it. Additionally all these debug f-strings are formatted eagerly even where the level does filter (main-process loops), ~1-2us/row.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a tiny configure_worker_logging() in logging_setup.py that does logger.remove() + re-adds stderr (and optionally the file sink) at os.environ['BALALAIKA_LOG_LEVEL'] (already exported by base.sh and inherited by spawn children), and call it first thing in every spawned worker entry: run_inference_worker-style functions, the three pool init_process functions, and as worker_init_fn for DataLoaders (or rely on inheritance once the GPU worker is fixed). Optionally guard the per-row debug calls with a precomputed `debug_on` bool or logger.opt(lazy=True) to skip eager f-string formatting.
findings.expected_gain: Minutes per GPU stage at 2M-file scale (4-6M stderr emits removed), correct log-level control in workers, DEBUG perf lines actually captured in the rotating file when enabled; seconds saved from eager f-string formatting
findings.risk: low — logging only; pipeline data outputs provably unchanged

findings.title: Dead `import torch` in src/utils/utils.py makes every CPU-only stage pay ~4s startup and ~500MB peak RSS
findings.description: src/utils/utils.py imports torch at module level but never uses it (grep confirms `torch` appears only on line 4). load_config/read_file_content live in this module, so every stage entry module imports it — including the six stages whose import chains are otherwise torch-free: download (0), distillmos_filter (5.5), antispoofing_filter (6.5), collate (12), to_webdataset (13), report (14). csv_manager/sidecars/audit do not import torch at module level (audit's torchaudio probe is lazy). Measured on this node, warm cache: `import torch` = 5.3s wall and 628MB peak RSS vs 1.5s/110MB for yaml+loguru+pandas — i.e. ~3.8s and ~500MB of pure waste per CPU-only stage process. The RSS matters most for collate/to_webdataset, which already hold multi-GB dataframes/metadata on this 31GB node.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete line 4 of src/utils/utils.py (also consider making `import torch` lazy inside src/utils/parallel.py, which only needs it for torch.cuda.device_count, though all its current callers import torch anyway). No vectorization/numba needed — pure dead-code removal.
findings.expected_gain: ~4s x 6 stages ≈ 23s per full pipeline run; ~500MB lower peak RSS in collate/to_webdataset/report/filter stages (first-class win on the 31GB node)
findings.risk: low — torch is unused in the module and torch-needing stages import it themselves; outputs unchanged

findings.title: cudf.pandas is blanket-injected into all 16 stages (GPU multiprocessing stages included) on cudf-equipped nodes
findings.description: base.sh probes `import cudf.pandas` once and, if present, launches EVERY stage via the runpy shim that calls cudf.pandas.install() in the stage parent. On this node cudf is not installed (verified), so today the cost is only the failed probe (~0.1s). But on any cudf-equipped node the design charges every stage parent a multi-second cuDF import plus a cuDF CUDA context (hundreds of MB of VRAM) — including the GPU multiprocessing stages (ASR, DistillMOS, antispoofing, denoising, music_detect) whose parents do little pandas work, and on a GPU shared with a training job that extra context is an OOM risk, not just overhead. It also now overlaps with the already-applied pyarrow CSV fast path in csv_manager (report.md §4.1): cudf.pandas intercepts pandas calls, so the optimized pandas/pyarrow path would be re-routed and its measured wins/memory profile no longer apply as benchmarked.
findings.proposed_fix: Gate the cudf prefix to an allowlist of CSV-bound stages (collate, distillmos_filter, antispoofing_filter, report) or flip the default to opt-in (BALALAIKA_ENABLE_CUDF=1), keeping BALALAIKA_DISABLE_CUDF for back-compat; fold the probe into the existing runtime_env python invocation so startup does one interpreter start instead of two.
findings.expected_gain: On cudf nodes: multi-second import + 300-800MB VRAM saved per GPU-stage parent and removal of an OOM failure mode; on this node: ~0.1s probe only
findings.risk: low on this node (no cudf → no behavior change); medium on cudf nodes — CSV ops move back to CPU pandas/pyarrow, values identical but stage wall-clock there changes; gate via config knob

findings.title: No thread-pool hygiene: ORT sessions default to physical-core intra-op pools with spinning; no OMP/BLAS caps exported for workers
findings.description: Nothing in the orchestration exports OMP_NUM_THREADS/OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS/MKL_NUM_THREADS (repo-wide grep: only torch.set_num_threads(1) in the crest worker_init), and most ORT sessions are created without thread settings: onnx_asr.load_model in transcription gets no sess_options (ORT default intra_op = physical cores = 24 here, inter-op 1+, busy-spin enabled), antispoofing builds SessionOptions but only sets graph_optimization_level. With 2 GPU workers per stage that is ~48 spinning ORT CPU threads per ASR/antispoofing stage that mostly wait on GPU EPs but burn cycles between Run() calls — on this box the CPU is shared with the user's training job and with up to 16 DataLoader workers per GPU worker plus pyarrow's own 48-thread pool in the parent during flushes. Denoising already demonstrates the fix pattern (ORT_THREADS=4 at denoising.py:69,169-170). torch DataLoader workers are safe (torch auto-caps to 1 thread in _worker_loop), so the exposure is ORT pools + any OpenBLAS use in forked workers (default pool = 48 threads per process, lazily created).
findings.proposed_fix: (a) Pass sess_options to onnx_asr.load_model (verified: signature accepts sess_options) and to the antispoofing session with intra_op_num_threads ≈ 2-4, inter_op_num_threads=1, and session config 'session.intra_op.allow_spinning'='0' for GPU-EP sessions; (b) add a runtime.threads_per_worker knob in runtime_env.py and export OMP_NUM_THREADS/OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS/MKL_NUM_THREADS/NUMEXPR_NUM_THREADS in base.sh run_python so all stage processes and their forked children inherit sane caps.
findings.expected_gain: 5-15% wall-clock on CPU-contended runs (this node always is, with the training job) and markedly less interference with the co-resident training; tens of MB of thread stacks saved per worker
findings.risk: medium — if some ASR/preprocessing ops actually execute on CPU, an aggressive intra_op cap can slow them (must measure per model); changing CPU-op thread counts can change float reduction order in CPU-executed kernels, so outputs need re-verification or a config knob defaulting to current behavior

findings.title: runtime_cfg/get_onnx_providers re-parse the YAML config and re-mkdir the TRT cache dir on every session build
findings.description: Every TRT-enabled session creation calls get_onnx_providers → runtime_cfg → _load_runtime, which re-opens and yaml.safe_load's the entire config plus Path.mkdir(parents=True) on the cache dir. Transcription does this once per model per GPU worker per run (5 models × 2 workers, workers respawned per model), antispoofing/denoising/sortformer once per worker; benchmarking/warmup loops call it repeatedly. The config is immutable during a run, so this is pure redundant parse + syscalls — small in absolute terms (~1-3ms + 2 syscalls per call, tens of calls per stage) but a free 2-line fix and it removes config-file IO from worker startup on the HDD-resident production checkouts.
findings.proposed_fix: functools.lru_cache on runtime_cfg keyed by the resolved config path (returning a copy or an immutable mapping to keep callers from mutating the cache), and cache the mkdir per (cache_path, cuda_id).
findings.expected_gain: Negligible wall-clock (<0.1s/stage); removes redundant config-file reads/mkdirs from every worker startup — cleanliness/IO-hygiene win
findings.risk: low — values are read-only during a run; outputs unchanged

areas.area_summary: Read base.sh, src/stage_runner.sh, src/utils/runtime_env.py, configs/config.yaml, src/utils/logging_setup.py end-to-end, plus the helpers they feed (gpu.py, parallel.py, utils.py, stage worker entries and dataset __getitem__ paths) and verified prior-pass items from .claude/analysis/orchestration.json against current code. Already fixed and NOT re-reported: cpu_affinity now defaults to "" with NUMA guidance (config.yaml:91-100), stage_runner.sh now mirrors base.sh's nvidia/TensorRT LD_LIBRARY_PATH (stage_runner.sh:35-47), and the flush_every_rows comment. The orchestration shell itself is thin and cheap; the real remaining costs are in process bootstrap: (1) spawned workers fall back to loguru's default DEBUG stderr sink (empirically verified), making per-row/per-file debug f-strings in hot loops emit at production scale; (2) a dead `import torch` in src/utils/utils.py charges ~4s + ~500MB peak RSS to six otherwise torch-free stages (measured 5.3s/628MB for torch import on this node); (3) the cudf.pandas blanket injection (inactive here — cudf absent — but a VRAM/OOM hazard and a pyarrow-path conflict on cudf nodes); (4) missing thread caps for ORT sessions and OMP/BLAS in a 48-thread box shared with the user's training job (denoising already caps to 4 threads, others don't; onnx_asr.load_model accepts sess_options so the fix is plumbing). Trivia merged here rather than as findings: base.sh does 3 interpreter cold-starts before any stage (runtime_env dump — which runs on the SYSTEM python3 before venv activation and thus needs system PyYAML, a portability footgun; the sys.version probe at base.sh:89 replaceable with python3 -V parsing; the cudf probe) totaling ~1-2s one-off; --strict mode spawns one python3 per stage just to read an int from stage_N_status.json (~15 cold starts, could be a sentinel file or grep); logging_setup.py:108 basicConfig(level=0) routes every stdlib record from every library through the intercept handler (record build + depth-6 frame walk) even when sinks reject it — should pass the resolved level; stage_to_units forks 3 subshells per stage gate (negligible); base.sh defaults stage=11/stop_stage=14 so a bare `bash base.sh` silently skips stages 0-10 (footgun, not perf). The fixed ~5-7s interpreter+framework import per stage launch (16 stages ≈ 1.5min/run) is structural and amortized at production scale; its only cheaply-removable component is the dead torch import (finding 2) — transcription's per-model worker respawn is already report.md §9.1 and was not re-reported.

findings.title: to_webdataset ships the FULL 2M-row metadata dict to every export worker (report claim not actually implemented) — OOM risk at 31 GB
findings.description: load_metadata() materializes one dict-of-dicts for the whole CSV (2M rows x ~15 metadata keys ≈ 2-3 GB of Python dicts), then main() passes that same full `metadata_dict` to EVERY ProcessPoolExecutor worker (executor.submit(worker_fn, ..., metadata_dict, ...) at line 212). Under spawn, each of the N workers pickles+unpickles its own complete copy: parent 3 GB + 4 workers x 3 GB ≈ 15 GB before any tar buffering — on the 31 GB node (≈21 GB free) this OOMs at production scale. IMPORTANT: report.md §8 claims 'webdataset workers now receive only their shard's metadata instead of the full dict' — the code shows this was never applied; the claim is currently false.
findings.proposed_fix: Each worker's chunk of audio paths determines exactly which stems it needs: before submit, compute `chunk_stems = {os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(p))[0] for p in chunk}` and pass `{k: metadata_dict[k] for k in chunk_stems & metadata_dict.keys()}`. Optionally also free the parent's full dict after slicing (per-chunk dicts are disjoint). No worker logic changes; identical tar/json output. Then fix the report.md §8 sentence.
findings.expected_gain: Worker-side RAM drops from N x full-dict to 1/N each (~8-12 GB saved at 2M rows, 4 workers); pickle volume per submit drops ~N x. Turns a near-certain OOM at 2M+ rows into a flat ~3-4 GB stage.
findings.risk: low — pure data-plumbing change; every worker receives a superset-equal view of the keys it can ever look up, so outputs are byte-identical. Pinned testable via existing tests/test_to_webdataset.py plus an RSS measurement on a synthetic 2M-row metadata fixture.

findings.title: Collate holds every sidecar text for the whole dataset in RAM 3x over (list-of-dicts + DataFrame + Arrow) — est. 12-18 GB peak at 2M rows
findings.description: Stage 12 accumulates ALL per-file sidecar texts into `results` (2M dicts x ~10 text fields x 200-600 B + ~640 B dict overhead ≈ 6-9 GB), then builds `extracted_df = pd.DataFrame(results)` while `results` is still alive, merges with the metadata frame into `final_df`, runs add_asr_consistency (which transiently concats 5 full text columns into one 10M-element series for factorize), and finally `to_parquet` converts everything into Arrow buffers (string data copied again, +4-7 GB transient). Estimated peak RSS 12-18 GB at 2M rows — borderline OOM with ~21 GB free, certain OOM at 4M+. The §4.2 optimizations fixed CPU (apply→vectorized, sidecar slab reads) but not this peak.
findings.proposed_fix: Stream instead of materialize: iterate the existing slabs in order, build a pandas/Arrow record batch per slab (merge with the corresponding metadata rows via an index on filepath), compute asr_consistency per batch (it is row-local — within-row equality of normalized transcripts; per-batch factorize codes preserve within-row equality, so results are identical), and append batches to one pyarrow.parquet.ParquetWriter with a fixed unified schema. Drop `results`/`extracted_df` entirely. executor.map already yields slabs in submission order, so row order in the parquet is unchanged.
findings.expected_gain: Peak RSS from O(dataset) to O(slab) — roughly 12-18 GB → 2-3 GB at 2M rows; unlocks 10M-row collation on this node. Wall time roughly unchanged (same reads, same factorize work in pieces).
findings.risk: medium — output parquet should be value-identical and row-order-identical, but the file's internal row-group layout changes and the unified schema must be fixed up-front (a late-appearing sidecar column would otherwise change dtypes). Pin with a round-trip comparison test on the existing bench_collate fixture (values + row order, not bytes).

findings.title: DistillMOS re-probes audio lengths SERIALLY per shard via torchaudio.info and thrashes a JSON sort cache, despite shards already being duration-sorted from the CSV
findings.description: main() already calls ensure_audio_durations (parallel probe + balalaika.csv total_duration cache) and prepare_length_bucketed_work_shards, so every shard file list is ALREADY sorted by duration. Yet _process_files builds the dataloader through sort_by_length(), which on the first shard probes every file with serial torchaudio.info under tqdm in the GPU worker, and on every SUBSEQUENT shard finds the JSON cache 'stale' (cached set = previous shard, disjoint from current) → re-probes its whole shard serially and rewrites distillmos_sorted_files.json on the production HDD. At 2M files / 10k shard_size = 200 shards x 10k random header reads (~3-8 ms each on cold HDD) ≈ 1.7-4.5 h of GPU-idle serial probing per run, plus 200 JSON rewrites and a write race between GPU workers sharing one cache file.
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the per-shard sort_by_length call (and the JSON cache): pass shard files to DistillMOSDataset in shard order, which is already duration-sorted by the same total_duration values. If exact batch-composition parity with current behavior is wanted, keep a knob `distillmos.sort_in_loader: true` defaulting to false, or sort by the durations dict already loaded in main (ship per-shard durations in the shard files as 'path\tduration' lines).
findings.expected_gain: Eliminates ~2M redundant HDD header reads + 200 JSON rewrites per run (hours of wall time at 2M files; ~50 s GPU idle per 10k shard). Zero extra RAM.
findings.risk: medium — per-file MOS scores can shift marginally for files whose batch neighbors change, because pad_sequence zero-padding depends on batch composition and the model gets no length mask; CSV durations are rounded to 4 dp vs probed exact floats, so within-bucket ordering can differ. Gate by the config knob and validate on the 250-wav bench sample (DistillMOS package + GPU are present).

findings.title: music_detect calls ensure_audio_durations PER SHARD inside every GPU worker — full-CSV duration scan + possible full-CSV rewrite under flock, 200x per run; the cache loader itself is a per-row Python loop
findings.description: Unlike distillmos/antispoofing/denoising (which ensure durations once in main), music_detect's _process_files runs ensure_audio_durations for every claimed shard: each call reads balalaika.csv's filepath+total_duration columns AND runs _csv_duration_cache's per-row Python loop (normalize_path_string + set lookup under tqdm) over ALL 2M rows (~4-7 s/call), per shard, per GPU worker → ~15-25 min of redundant CSV parsing at 200 shards, all competing for the same file. Worse, any shard with rows missing total_duration triggers upsert_columns — a FULL CSV read+merge+rewrite under the global flock from inside a GPU worker, serializing both workers and the periodic merger. create_loader also re-downloads/parses AutoFeatureExtractor.from_pretrained and respawns loader workers per shard.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist ensure_audio_durations to main() (exactly like distillmos_process.py:286), write 'path\tduration' shard lines or pass a per-shard durations dict, and instantiate AutoFeatureExtractor once per worker. Independently, vectorize _csv_duration_cache: `s = _normalize_path_series(df['filepath']); m = s.isin(requested_norms)` then dict(zip(s[m], durations[m])) — removes the 2M-iteration loop for every stage that uses durations (distillmos, antispoofing, denoising, transcription x5 models).
findings.expected_gain: music_detect: removes ~200 full-CSV scans + any mid-stage full-CSV rewrites (tens of minutes + large HDD I/O at 2M rows). Vectorized cache loader: ~4-7 s → <1 s per call, x (number of duration-using stage launches, ≈9 per pipeline run).
findings.risk: low — same durations end up in the same shards; per-shard skip logic unchanged. The music model file is absent on this node so only the duration-layer half is micro-benchmarkable here (2M-row CSV fixture exists); the hoist is structurally identical to the distillmos pattern already validated.

findings.title: Periodic CSV flush amplification: full-CSV rewrite every 600 s on long stages, plus a full existence re-scan (drop_missing_files) and 2M-path bootstrap re-normalization on EVERY flush — all redundant for crash safety
findings.description: PeriodicCsvMerger flushes whenever 100k new rows OR 600 s elapse with any new rows (config flush_every_seconds: 600). Each flush = read all partials + read full main CSV + outer merge + rewrite full CSV (~15 s CPU and ~0.3-0.5 GB write at 2M rows). A 10 h stage → ~60 flushes ≈ 15 min CPU and ~20-30 GB of HDD reads+writes. Crest and music additionally pass drop_missing_files=True to the MERGER, so every flush also runs _paths_exist_mask over all 2M rows — one scandir per unique directory (~50k dirs on HDD, several minutes each flush, cache rebuilt from scratch per flush). preprocess_existing_chunks passes bootstrap_audio_paths to the merger, so every flush re-normalizes + concats + dedupes all 2M bootstrap paths. None of this is needed for crash safety: partials are never deleted by the merger and the post-stage absorb_partial_csvs replays everything — periodic flushes only keep balalaika.csv 'fresh' for observers.
findings.proposed_fix: (a) Default flush_every_seconds much higher (e.g. 3600) or 0, keeping flush_every_rows as the only trigger, and document that resume safety comes from partials. (b) Stop passing drop_missing_files=True and bootstrap_audio_paths into PeriodicCsvMerger (keep both on the final absorb only) — final CSV state is identical because the final absorb prunes/bootstraps anyway. (c) Optionally make _count_partial_rows incremental (remember per-file offset, count only appended bytes).
findings.expected_gain: Removes ~50-90% of mid-stage CSV I/O on long runs: tens of GB of HDD writes and many full-tree scandir passes per filter stage at 2M rows; scales linearly with dataset size. Final balalaika.csv provably unchanged.
findings.risk: low for (b)/(c) — final-state equality is exact (absorb does the same work once). Low-medium for (a): mid-run balalaika.csv is staler, and a crash window of up to flush interval relies on partial-absorb at next start (already the designed recovery path). All gated by existing csv: config knobs.

findings.title: accents/phonemizer submit one ProcessPool future per FILE: ~2M Future+WorkItem objects (~1-2 GB) in the parent plus per-item pickling; punctuation's slab pattern not propagated
findings.description: run_per_gpu_pool submits every item as its own future and keeps a future_to_item dict over all of them; as_completed additionally registers waiters on each. Stage 8 (punctuation) was converted to batch_size slabs in the prior pass, but accents (stage 9) and phonemizer (stage 10) still pass the raw pending-file list: at 2M pending files that is ~2M Future + _WorkItem + dict entries ≈ 1-2 GB parent RSS, 2M individual pickle/IPC round trips (~20-30 µs each ≈ 1-2 min pure dispatch), and a tqdm update per item. Supporting RAM costs in the same path: pending lists are built as Path objects (~2-3x the bytes of plain strings, several hundred MB at 2M), and DirNameCache._names grows unbounded — it retains every directory's full name set including all sidecars (~10-12M names ≈ 1-1.5 GB) for the whole scan even though inputs arrive grouped by directory.
findings.proposed_fix: Slab accents and phonemizer exactly like punctuation.py:121-124 (work_fn loops over a list; per-file error handling stays inside the slab fn — outputs unchanged since both work_fns are independent per file). Return str paths instead of Path from pending scans. Bound DirNameCache with a small LRU (e.g. keep last 64 dirs — inputs from CSV order are directory-grouped, so hit rate stays ~100%).
findings.expected_gain: Parent RSS during stages 9/10 drops by ~2-4 GB at 2M files; dispatch overhead shrinks ~slab_size x (minutes saved); behavior identical. LRU bound caps scan RAM at ~MBs instead of ~1.5 GB.
findings.risk: low — same function applied per file inside slabs; punctuation already validated this pattern end-to-end. LRU eviction only costs re-scandir on out-of-order inputs (correctness unchanged).

findings.title: preprocess (stage 1) accumulates every chunk-metadata row in BOTH the GPU worker and the parent, only to compute len() and a duration sum — rows are already persisted via partial CSVs
findings.description: _run_diarization_shard appends every written segment dict to `results` for the lifetime of the worker (line 793) even though each row was just streamed to preprocess_part_<gpu>.csv; process_gpu_batch then pickles the whole list back through future.result() (line 816), and main() extends `all_results` with everything (line 935). The ONLY uses are hours_out = sum(total_duration) and files_out = len(all_results) (lines 961-967). At ~2M chunks x ~10-key dict ≈ 2-4 GB held in the parent plus a matching transient copy during IPC unpickling — on top of the diarization dataloader's prefetched full-length podcasts (a 2 h source decoded to 16 kHz float32 is ~0.45 GB per item x workers x prefetch_factor).
findings.proposed_fix: Have _run_diarization_shard return only {chunk_count, duration_sum_s, crest_audit}; replace all_results with two accumulators. (Equivalent data for the audit could also be derived from the final absorb's partials_df, like crest does.) One-line semantics, no output change to balalaika.csv or filter_summary.csv values.
findings.expected_gain: ~2-4 GB parent + ~2-4 GB transient IPC RAM removed at 2M chunks; removes a multi-GB pickle between processes. Matters most exactly when stage 1 runs at full dataset scale on this 31 GB node.
findings.risk: low — record_stage_summary receives identical numbers (sum/len are computed from the same rows). Not end-to-end benchmarkable on this node (sortformer/SmartTurn ONNX absent), but the accumulation cost is provable with a synthetic row generator.

findings.title: Transcription get_valid_paths and ROVER pending scan stat every audio file individually (per model pass) instead of using the existing DirNameCache — 10M+ random HDD stats per pipeline run
findings.description: Stage 7 runs get_valid_paths once per ASR model (5x): for each of 2M audio paths it calls text_sidecar_complete(sidecar) → Path.exists() + stat (one or two syscalls per file, random order on the HDD), i.e. ~2M stats x 5 models = 10M syscalls; cold-cache HDD at 3-8 ms/seek is potentially hours for the first pass, and consensus mode adds up to len(processed) more existence probes + file reads per candidate. ROVER's _pending_audio_paths repeats the same per-file pattern for _rover.txt. The prior pass already built DirNameCache (one scandir per directory, validated identical pending sets) and applied it to stages 8-10 (report §4.7) — these two scans were missed.
findings.proposed_fix: Use DirNameCache in get_valid_paths and ROVERWrapper._pending_audio_paths (and check_consensus's existence probes) — same pattern as sidecars.pending_audio_to_sidecar. One semantic edge to preserve/document: text_sidecar_complete(retry_empty=True) needs st_size, which scandir entries provide via entry.stat() from the same directory read (still batched); ENAMETOOLONG names become 'missing' instead of 'complete' (the same documented trade already accepted for stages 8-10).
findings.expected_gain: Pending-scan syscalls drop from O(files x models) to O(directories x models): ~10M stats → ~250k scandirs at 2M files/50k dirs. On the production HDD this is the difference between hours and minutes for cold scans; on warm caches it is still a 40x syscall reduction.
findings.risk: low — equivalence of DirNameCache vs per-file exists was already verified on a 50k fixture for the sibling stages; ASR models for stage 7 are present locally so the scan phase can be timed end-to-end on the bench sample.

areas.area_summary: Cross-cutting RAM/I-O sweep over all of src/ (csv_manager, work_shards, parallel, audio_durations, sidecars, audit, all dataset loaders, stages 1-13 incl. download/report/recovery skimmed). The CSV state layer is genuinely well-optimized after the prior pass (pyarrow read/write, hardlink .bak, vectorized normalization, scandir caches) — the remaining material problems are (1) whole-dataset materialization in parents (collate ~12-18 GB peak, webdataset full-metadata-dict-per-worker which report.md §8 incorrectly claims is fixed, preprocess all_results), (2) per-shard redundant work inside GPU workers (distillmos serial length re-probe + JSON cache thrash, music_detect full-CSV duration scan per shard), and (3) avoidable mid-stage I/O amplification (600 s full-CSV rewrite flushes plus drop_missing/bootstrap work per flush that the final absorb redoes anyway; per-file stat storms in transcription/rover pending scans). Estimated peak RSS at 2M rows: collate 12-18 GB and webdataset 12-15 GB are the two OOM candidates on 31 GB; heavy filter stages sit at ~5-8 GB (upsert outer-merge + Arrow write transients, acceptable but worth watching at 10M rows where Parquet state — already on the report's next-steps list — becomes necessary). Minor items folded here rather than reported: PartialCsvWriter still flushes per row (~2M small write syscalls/stage, seconds — page cache absorbs it); _count_partial_rows re-reads whole partials each 30 s poll (could be offset-incremental); claim_work_shard does a sorted glob per claim (trivial at ≤2000 shards); every dataset __getitem__ builds loguru debug f-strings + 2 perf_counter calls even at INFO level (~10-30 s/2M files); each per-shard DataLoader respawn under spawn costs ~4-6 s torch import per loader worker (~10-25 min/stage at 200 shards — fixable by an iterable dataset fed from claimed shards, medium-risk restructure); preprocess_existing_chunks' fused path decodes each chunk twice (16 kHz for diarization + native for crest/loudness — fixing would alter diarization input, output-changing); antispoofing decodes+resamples full files for a fixed 64600-sample random crop (output-changing to fix, left alone). unprocessed_paths/ensure_main_csv still read all CSV columns where 2 would do (folded into the flush finding's state-layer scope; ~1-2 GB and ~2 s saved per stage start). numba 0.65.1 is installed but none of these wins need it — they are structural I/O/RAM fixes, not kernel rewrites.

Stage 7 (transcription.py + datasets/transcription.py) and ROVER (rover.py) were read end-to-end together with every helper they call (work_shards, audio_durations, parallel, gpu, sidecars, csv_manager) and the third-party hot paths (onnx_asr adapters/asr.py/gigaam.py/kaldi.py/preprocessors, crowdkit ROVER). The report.md §9.1 lead is confirmed and quantified: each of the 5 configured models re-decodes every file from HDD (datasets/transcription.py:21) and respawns GPU workers (transcription.py:378), but two larger un-reported costs were found: the RNNT/Kaldi greedy decode runs 200+ batch=1 ONNX session calls per file (why giga_rnnt is 7.3 it/s vs ~60 for CTC — it sets the stage critical path), and crowdkit ROVER is a pure-Python DP with deepcopy in the backtrace (prime numba candidate; numba 0.65.1 is installed). ROVER shards are disk-claimed, not pickled, so the 'shards pickled to rover workers' concern is a non-issue; workers re-read sidecars from disk themselves (covered in finding 4). VAD path is config-off (use_vad: False) — if enabled, segment-iterator results would misalign with paths in save_results (zip truncates silently) — pre-existing, flagged in .claude/analysis/transcription.json. Other trivia not worth findings: processed/errors mp.Value '+=' is non-atomic across 2 GPU workers (undercount only, transcription.py:147/171/184); vosk & vosk_small share one sidecar suffix (already in report §6 known issues); np.asarray(waveforms.numpy()) rewrap is a no-op; claim_work_shard's O(S²) glob is negligible at ~200 shards; pin_memory=False is correct here (inputs go to ORT as numpy); ROVER stats-queue drain with 1s timeout can undercount logged stats only. All 5 ASR models (gigaam-v3 ctc/e2e/rnnt, vosk, t-one) are present locally, so findings 1, 2, 6, 7 are end-to-end benchmarkable on the 250-wav bench sample; 3-5 are benchmarkable with CPU fixtures.

findings.title: 5x redundant audio decode + HDD re-read: share one decode across all ASR models
findings.description: The per-model loop (transcription.py:339) re-runs the entire pipeline for each of the 5 configured models: TranscriptionDataset.__getitem__ does a full torchcodec decode + mono mixdown + resample for every file on every model pass (datasets/transcription.py:21-26), and run_per_gpu_processes spawns fresh per-GPU processes per model (5 spawns x 2 GPUs, each paying torch import + CUDA context + ORT/TRT session init, ~15-60 s each). 4 of 5 models want 16 kHz; tone wants 8 kHz (verified onnx_asr/models/tone.py:48). On the production HDD (/mnt/hdd_6tb_1) each audio file (~100-500 KB) is randomly read 5x; 31 GB RAM cannot page-cache a production dataset, so at 1M files the 4 redundant passes cost roughly 4M extra seeks+reads (~5-15 ms each cold = 11-33 disk-hours) plus 4M redundant decodes (~5-15 ms CPU each, ~11-17 CPU-hours spread over 16 loader workers). This is the report.md §9.1 item, now concretized.
findings.proposed_fix: Restructure to one long-lived worker per GPU that loads all 5 models (config-gated, e.g. transcription.share_decode: true): DataLoader decodes each file ONCE at native rate; the worker resamples each batch from native to 16 kHz (gigaam x3 + vosk) and to 8 kHz (tone) — resampling from native per target keeps inputs bit-identical to today's per-model resample — then runs the models sequentially on the same batch, writing each model's sidecar as now. Per-model skip/consensus masks are computed per item: pending-per-model from one sidecar scan, and consensus for models 4-5 evaluated in-memory from texts produced in the same pass (also kills finding 4's consensus re-reads). VRAM for 5 resident ONNX sessions is ~2-4 GB — gate the knob and fall back to the current per-model loop on VRAM-poor nodes. No numba/CUDA needed; this is orchestration.
findings.expected_gain: Removes ~80% of stage-7 audio IO and decode CPU and 4 of 5 worker spawn cycles; on the HDD production node, estimated 2-4x stage wall-clock once the RNNT decode bottleneck (finding 2) is also addressed; at minimum tens of disk-hours per 1M files.
findings.risk: medium — structural change to stage 7; outputs provably unchanged per model on the 250-wav sample (same decode + same per-target resample + same models), consensus semantics preserved by evaluating the same rule in-memory; gate behind a config knob with the current path as fallback.

findings.title: RNNT/Kaldi greedy decode: 200+ sequential batch=1 ONNX calls per file (giga_rnnt is the stage critical path)
findings.description: _AsrWithTransducerDecoding._decoding loops file-by-file and frame-by-frame: for gigaam-v3 RNNT (subsampling_factor=4, 25 frames/s) an 8 s clip does >=200 joiner.run() calls plus a decoder.run() per emitted token, each a tiny CUDA/TRT session call with H2D copy + launch + D2H sync (~100-300 us each, so ~50-120 ms/file of pure round-trip overhead). I verified the gigaam decoder/joint graphs have FIXED batch=1 input dims (x:[1,1], h/c:[1,1,320], enc:[1,768,1]), while vosk's decoder/joiner have dynamic batch 'N'. This is exactly why warmup measured giga_rnnt at 7.3 it/s with batching HURTING, vs ~60 it/s for the CTC models: at 1M files giga_rnnt alone is ~38 GPU-hours vs ~4.5 h per CTC model — it sets the whole stage's critical path and the shared-decode win (finding 1) is capped by it.
findings.proposed_fix: Three tiers, in-repo via a small onnx_asr subclass/post-load patch (precedent: fast_g2p): (a) LOW RISK: after onnx_asr.load_model, rebuild the decoder and joiner InferenceSessions on CPUExecutionProvider only (inputs/outputs are tiny numpy arrays already; 320/768-dim matmuls run in ~10-30 us on CPU, eliminating per-step GPU round trips) — keep the encoder on GPU; (b) vosk: batch the greedy loop across the batch dimension (graphs are dynamic-'N'; process all items' frame t together with done-masks, preserving per-item emission logic); (c) gigaam: ONNX dim surgery to make batch dynamic (LSTM + joint ops are batch-agnostic), then batched decode as in (b). Verify transcripts byte-identical on the 250-wav sample (fp32 CPU vs fp32 GPU argmax-tie risk, same class of check as the G2P TF32 work).
findings.expected_gain: (a) alone: estimated 2-5x on giga_rnnt and vosk (the two slowest models, 7.3 and 7.4-10.3 it/s); (b)+(c): 5-15x, pulling giga_rnnt off the critical path. At 1M files this is tens of GPU-hours per run.
findings.risk: medium — (a) is a provider swap with output verification needed (argmax ties); (b)/(c) reimplement greedy decode and must reproduce emission/step semantics exactly; all verifiable transcript-for-transcript on local models.

findings.title: ROVER alignment is pure-Python DP with deepcopy backtrace — numba rewrite candidate
findings.description: crowdkit ROVER._align fills an O(n*m) DP table in pure Python: per cell it allocates 3-4 option tuples, does dict-key membership on word strings, and min(options, key=lambda) — ~3-6 us/cell; the backtrace then does deepcopy(ref_edges) (a dict of attr-class AlignmentEdge objects) for EVERY aligned token position. Per task (5 hypotheses, ~15-30 words for 5-15 s clips): 4 alignment passes x ~400-900 cells + ~25 deepcopies = ~5-15 ms, plus pandas groupby overhead. At 1M files that is ~2-4 CPU-hours through rover_workers=4 (~30-60 min wall), all GC-churning Python. numba 0.65.1 is confirmed installed in .dev_venv.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace crowdkit with an in-repo ROVER: per task, map tokens to int ids; run the DP cost matrix + action backtrace in an @njit kernel over int32 arrays (alignment-set membership becomes a per-column int-id set encoded as a small sorted array or bitmask over the <=5-model vocab of that column); build the word-transition network in Python from the returned action sequence WITHOUT deepcopy (construct fresh dicts). Must preserve crowdkit's exact tie-breaking: min() takes the FIRST minimal option in order (CORRECT/SUBSTITUTION, DELETION, INSERTION) and _get_result's max() orders by (sources_count, len(word), word). Validate output-identical against crowdkit on real per-model sidecars from the bench sample plus edge cases (empty texts, single hypothesis, ties).
findings.expected_gain: 10-50x on the aggregation phase (even a no-numba rewrite that drops attr objects, per-cell tuple allocs and deepcopy gives ~5-10x); ~30-60 min saved per 1M-file run, more if rover_workers is then reduced to free CPU for ASR loader workers.
findings.risk: low-medium — outputs must match crowdkit exactly including tie-breaks; fully testable offline on CPU, no model involvement.

findings.title: Sidecar stat/read storm: pending scans, consensus re-reads, and ROVER's double pass ignore the existing DirNameCache
findings.description: get_valid_paths probes each file's sidecar with text_sidecar_complete (exists + stat with retry_empty=True) and then RE-stats the same sidecar (exists + stat again) just to count retries (transcription.py:294-296) — 2-4 syscalls per file PER MODEL (x5). check_consensus (models idx>=3, i.e. vosk and tone with consensus_num=3) additionally opens and fully reads 3-4 prior ~200-byte .txt sidecars per pending file, with no early exit once consensus is provable. ROVER then does ANOTHER full stat pass (_pending_audio_paths) followed by a second pass doing path_exists + open/read for all 5 model sidecars per file. At 1M files: ~15-20M metadata syscalls + ~7M tiny-file random reads; on the cold production HDD this is hours of seeking before/around the actual GPU work. Report §4.7's DirNameCache (one scandir per directory) was applied to the text stages' pending() helper but transcription and rover never use it.
findings.proposed_fix: Extend DirNameCache (src/utils/sidecars.py:34) with an optional size map (entry.stat() during the scandir — directory-local, sequential) and use it in get_valid_paths (resolving existence + zero-size in one directory pass per model, dropping the redundant re-stat) and in ROVER, merging _pending_audio_paths and _records_for_audio_paths into ONE directory-ordered walk that resolves rover-pending and reads the 5 model sidecars in the same visit. Add an early exit in check_consensus when any text count reaches consensus_num. Note the cache must be rebuilt per model pass (earlier models write sidecars that later passes must see) — still 5 readdir sweeps instead of ~20M random stats. With finding 1's shared pass, the consensus reads disappear entirely (texts are in memory).
findings.expected_gain: 2-4x fewer syscalls plus sequential directory locality instead of random seeks; on HDD, stage-start scans drop from hours to tens of minutes at 1M files; identical pending sets (same equivalence check as report §4.7).
findings.risk: low — same pattern already validated for text stages; pending-set equality is directly testable on a fixture tree.

findings.title: Per-model repeated CSV reads, per-row Python duration-cache loop, and Path-object/dict RAM doubling
findings.description: Inside the per-model loop, discover_audio_paths re-reads balalaika.csv for every model and once more for ROVER (6 reads x ~4.6 s at 2M rows), and ensure_audio_durations re-reads the full CSV AND re-normalizes every filepath in a per-row Python loop under tqdm (audio_durations.py:72-83 — the exact pattern report §4.1 vectorized elsewhere) for every model (5 x ~15-25 s at 2M rows), plus a locked full-CSV upsert writeback per model when any duration was probed. The durations are identical across models. RAM side: get_valid_paths materializes a list[Path] for the entire dataset per model (transcription.py:283, ~200-400 B/object), and ensure_audio_durations returns its dict keyed by BOTH original and normalized strings (audio_durations.py:174-179 — 2x entries, hundreds of MB at production scale on a 31 GB node), which is then held alongside the buckets dict during shard prep.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist to main() before the model loop: one discover_audio_paths call and one ensure_audio_durations call over the union of paths (durations don't change between models); reuse for ROVER. Replace the per-row loop in _csv_duration_cache with the existing vectorized normalize_path_values (csv_manager.py:109) over the column. Keep paths as strings in get_valid_paths (sidecar names via string ops, precedent: csv_manager string-ops dedupe) and return only normalized keys from ensure_audio_durations (callers already normalize). Pure caching/vectorization, no model involvement.
findings.expected_gain: ~2-4 min per run at 2M rows (linear in dataset size — ~10-20 min at 10M); a few hundred MB lower peak RAM at production scale, which matters at 31 GB total.
findings.risk: low — outputs unchanged (same paths, same durations); testable with the existing 2M-row CSV fixture from bench_csv_ops.

findings.title: DataLoader (16 workers) rebuilt for every 10k-file shard — GPU idles between shards
findings.description: _process_files constructs a brand-new DataLoader per claimed shard, so its 16 loader workers (config num_workers: 16) are torn down and respawned every 10k files even though persistent_workers=True is set (it only persists within one loader's lifetime). At 2M files that is 200 shards/model x 5 models = 1000 loader builds, each stalling the GPU ~0.5-2 s for worker startup + first-batch prefetch warmup, i.e. ~8-30 min of GPU idle per full run, plus ~16k worker process forks competing with the user's training job for the 48 CPU threads.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep ONE DataLoader alive per (worker, model): wrap shard claiming in an IterableDataset whose iterator claims shards via claim_work_shard and yields file paths continuously (claim coordination in the worker process, mark_work_shard_done driven from the consuming loop via shard-boundary sentinels in the stream); or simpler, have the GPU worker pre-claim all its shards' paths into one Dataset when shard counts are modest. Per-file error reporting (load_errors tuples in collate) is unaffected. Subsumed by finding 1's restructure if that is done first — implement there rather than twice.
findings.expected_gain: ~8-30 min per full 5-model run at 2M files; grows linearly with shard count; also smooths CPU load (no fork storms).
findings.risk: medium — multiprocessing/ordering complexity around shard-done marking and crash-resume semantics (a shard must only be marked done after all its files were attempted); outputs unchanged.

findings.title: TRT misconfiguration for ASR: profile max batch 16 vs auto batch sizes, TRT-first on per-step decoder/joiner, un-gated fp16 for transcripts
findings.description: transcription.use_tensorrt: True sends the TRT-first provider list from get_onnx_providers to EVERY onnx_asr session. Three issues: (1) onnx_asr pins encoder TRT profiles to batch<=16 / <=30 s (TensorRtOptions.profile_max_shapes) — the node profile recommends tone at batch 64 (report §2, 'still climbing'), so batch_size: auto would push shapes outside the profile and force multi-minute TRT engine rebuilds mid-run (and ORT caches engines per shape range, so each new range rebuilds); (2) the RNNT/Kaldi decoder and joiner sessions are created with the raw providers and NO profile (gigaam.py:79-80) — TRT engine builds for trivial [1,1]-shaped per-step graphs buy nothing and add startup cost (interacts with finding 2: these sessions should be CPU); (3) runtime.trt_fp16: True is a flag shared with Spectra-0, so ASR encoders run fp16 — onnx_asr even carries a workaround (use_low_precision clamps encoder_out_lens, asr.py:199-201) — meaning transcripts can diverge from CUDA-EP fp32 with no transcription-specific knob or documented check (report §7 validated fp16 fidelity for Spectra only).
findings.proposed_fix: In get_onnx_providers (or a transcription-specific wrapper): pass trt_profile_min/opt/max_shapes derived from the RESOLVED per-model batch size and max_bucket_duration instead of relying on onnx_asr defaults (onnx_asr merges caller provider options over its defaults — verified in update_onnx_providers); exclude TRT (and CUDA) from decoder/joiner sessions as part of finding 2(a); add transcription.trt_fp16 (default False until transcripts are verified identical-or-documented on the 250-wav sample) instead of inheriting the global flag.
findings.expected_gain: Avoids per-shape-range TRT engine rebuild stalls (minutes each, per model per GPU) once batch_size: auto is used; removes pointless engine builds for per-step graphs; makes the existing fp16 transcript divergence explicit and gated.
findings.risk: low for the profile/provider plumbing (behavior-preserving); the fp16 default change is a config-semantics decision — flag it, with the verification run as evidence either way.

findings.title: ROVER word-alignment DP runs in pure Python with attrs objects and deepcopy backtrace (crowdkit) — prime numba candidate
findings.description: Stage 7's ROVER aggregation calls crowdkit's _align once per extra hypothesis per file (5 ASR models => 4 alignments/file). The DP fills an O(N*M) table where every cell builds a 3-element options list of nested tuples, allocates AlignmentEdge attr-objects, and calls min(options, key=lambda) (rover.py:184-235); the backtrace then deepcopy()s a dict of attr objects per aligned position (rover.py:247). For typical <=15 s chunks (~30-50 words) that is ~10-12k DP cells * ~2-4 us/cell + ~100 deepcopies * 10-30 us = roughly 30-50 ms of pure Python per audio file, i.e. ~4-7 CPU-hours per 500k files — the single largest remaining Python compute kernel in the pipeline that report.md has not touched (transcription.json flagged ROVER's sidecar I/O, not the DP itself). The recurrence is data-dependent in both axes, so numpy cannot vectorize it; this is exactly the true-scalar-loop case where numba wins.
findings.proposed_fix: Vendor a fast ROVER into src/transcription/ (config-gated): (1) tokenize and intern words to int ids per task; (2) precompute the bool membership matrix is_hyp_in_ref[i,j] and has_empty[j] with Python sets (cheap); (3) run the DP cost table + int8 backpointers in a numba @njit kernel, reproducing min()'s tie-break order exactly (CORRECT/SUBSTITUTION preferred over DELETION over INSERTION at equal cost — min returns the first minimum); (4) backtrace in njit returning action codes, then rebuild the WTN edge dicts in Python replacing deepcopy with a shallow dict rebuild ({w: Edge(w, e.sources_count)}). Pin equivalence with tests comparing aggregated texts against stock crowdkit on varied fixtures (ties, empty transcripts, single-model tasks). numba 0.65.1 is already installed in .dev_venv, so no new dependency.
findings.expected_gain: DP cell cost drops from ~2-4 us to ~10-20 ns (100x+ on the DP); end-to-end ROVER stage compute ~10-30x. At 500k files: ~4-7 CPU-hours -> ~15-30 CPU-minutes; also less GC pressure in rover worker processes.
findings.risk: medium — output must stay character-identical; tie-break order and the ''-edge sources_count accounting must be replicated bit-for-bit, so gate behind a config knob and pin with A/B tests against stock crowdkit (pure CPU, fully testable on this node).

findings.title: pyloudnorm gating loop squares every sample 4x in a per-block Python loop — vectorizable with bitwise-identical output
locations: src/preprocess/preprocess.py:396-417,499-515 (called once per produced chunk in fused preprocessing)
findings.description: integrated_loudness computes gated blocks with 75% overlap in a nested Python loop: each 0.4 s block re-runs np.square over its slice, so every sample is squared 4x and a fresh ~17k-element temp is allocated per block (~150 blocks for a 15 s chunk). The block stats and both threshold passes then go through per-block scalar numpy list comprehensions. Per produced chunk this is ~2-5 ms of avoidable work + allocator churn inside the stage-1 GPU workers' serial path (loudness runs between decode and encode for every kept chunk); at 500k chunks that is ~20-40 CPU-minutes per full run. preprocess-vad-postprocess.json flagged 'replace pyloudnorm gating' but the key constraint — report.md S5 pinned byte-identical normalized audio — was not addressed there.
findings.proposed_fix: Vendor/monkeypatch a fast integrated_loudness used by audio_postprocessing.py: square each channel ONCE into a contiguous array, then z[i,j] = np.sum(squared[l:u]) — same elements, same order, same dtype => numpy pairwise summation is bitwise identical to the current np.sum(np.square(block)), so the measured LUFS and the normalized audio bytes cannot change. Optionally go further with sliding_window_view(squared, win)[::step].sum(axis=-1) and vectorized log10 over the block array (verify bit-identity in a test; fall back to the square-once variant if any file differs). Pure numpy beats numba here (simple reductions).
findings.expected_gain: integrated_loudness block section ~5-10x faster (~2-5 ms -> ~0.3-0.5 ms per chunk); ~20-40 CPU-minutes saved per 500k-chunk run plus reduced allocation churn in preprocess workers. Small but zero-risk in its square-once form.
findings.risk: low — square-once variant is provably bitwise identical; the fully vectorized variant changes summation order (1-ulp class) and should be gated behind a verified test against the 250-file bench sample (report.md already has the byte-identity harness for loudness output).

findings.title: Sortformer CPU post-processing kernels (_binarize, silence-profile running mean, spkcache gather/topk) are scalar Python loops
locations: src/preprocess/sortformer_onnx.py:253-260 (_update_silence_profile: sequential running mean over up to 124 frames)
src/preprocess/sortformer_onnx.py:310-316 (_boost_topk_scores: full argsort where argpartition suffices)
findings.description: Refines report.md S9.2 (analyzed, not applied) with numba-vs-numpy verdicts and kernel-level benchmarkability. _binarize iterates preds[t,spk] frame by frame: ~45k frames/hour x 4 speakers = 180k numpy scalar indexings (~60-120 ms CPU per audio-hour). _update_silence_profile updates a 512-dim running mean one frame at a time (up to 124 sequential vector ops per FIFO pop, ~0.5-1 ms per ~10 s chunk). _get_topk_indices/_gather_spkcache loop over the 188-slot cache per compression. All are minor vs ONNX inference but execute serially in the stage-1 GPU worker between model calls, and all are pure-numpy functions that can be unit-benchmarked on this node with synthetic preds/embs arrays even though the sortformer .onnx itself is absent.
findings.proposed_fix: (a) _binarize: with the default onset==offset==0.5 (DiarizationConfig defaults are used at preprocess.py:103) the hysteresis degenerates to a plain threshold — vectorize with np.flatnonzero(np.diff(np.concatenate([[0], (preds[:,spk]>=0.5).view(np.int8), [0]]))) to get rise/fall indices; keep a numba @njit fallback for onset!=offset (true hysteresis is sequential, numba's case). The merge/min-duration/pad passes stay as-is (interval lists are short). (b) _update_silence_profile: keep the exact sequential float32 update but inside a numba @njit loop — numba reproduces the scalar op order bit-exactly, unlike a batched (mean*n + sum)/ (n+k) rewrite which drifts at ~1e-7 and would perturb spkcache embeddings fed back into the model. (c) _gather_spkcache: replace the row loop with fancy indexing (np.where on the disabled mask); _boost_topk_scores: np.argpartition instead of full argsort. (d) is identical-output; verify (a)/(c) with property tests over random preds.
findings.expected_gain: ~0.1-0.2 s of worker CPU per audio-hour returned to the decode/VAD path; matters when sortformer ONNX runs fast on GPU and CPU post-proc serializes the loop. Kernel-level: _binarize ~50-100x, gather ~20x.
findings.risk: low for _binarize/_gather/_boost (bit-identical outputs, property-testable); the silence-profile change is only safe in the numba bit-exact form — flag the batched variant as output-changing. End-to-end stage validation impossible on this node (model file absent), kernel-level benchmarks fully possible.

findings.title: get_chunk_metrics is O(final_chunks x raw_segments) per episode — full diarization segment scan per chunk
locations: src/preprocess/preprocess.py:463-472 (called once per final segment inside cut_audio)
findings.description: For every cut chunk, get_chunk_metrics linearly scans ALL raw diarization segments of the whole episode (line 304) to find overlaps. A 2 h episode with ~2-3k raw segments and ~500-700 final chunks does 1-2 M Python iterations (~0.3-0.6 s) — quadratic in episode length and executed serially inside the stage-1 GPU worker between chunk decodes/writes. At ~10k long episodes this is 1-2 CPU-hours and adds directly to per-file wall time since nothing overlaps it.
findings.proposed_fix: raw_segments is already sorted by start (diarize_audio returns sorted). Precompute numpy arrays starts/ends once per episode plus a running prefix-max of ends; per chunk use np.searchsorted(starts, c_end) to bound candidates and the prefix-max to skip segments ending before c_start, then build the small overlap list and KEEP the existing Python merge/sum/round logic over it so the arithmetic (and round(x,2) outputs) stays exactly identical. Pure numpy/bisect; numba unnecessary.
findings.expected_gain: 20-30x on this function: ~0.3-0.6 s -> ~10-20 ms per 2 h episode; total ~1-2 CPU-hours saved per 10k-episode run, all of it on the stage-1 critical path.
findings.risk: low — selection is changed, arithmetic is not; equivalence checkable with property tests on random segment sets (this is CPU-only, no model needed).
