findings.title: audio_durations._csv_duration_cache walks the full main CSV row-by-row under tqdm; transcription re-runs it per ASR model, music_detect per shard
locations: src/utils/audio_durations.py:72-88 (per-row loop: normalize_path_string + tqdm per row)
src/transcription/transcription.py:352-356 (ensure_audio_durations inside the per-model loop => 5x per run)
src/separation/music_detect.py:126-130 (ensure_audio_durations inside _process_files => once per claimed shard, full CSV re-read each time)
findings.description: ensure_audio_durations reads filepath/total_duration from balalaika.csv and then loops over every row in Python calling normalize_path_string and updating tqdm (lines 72-82). At 2 M rows that is ~5-10 s of pure Python per call — and it is called once per ASR model in transcription (5x) and once per work shard in music_detect (each call also re-parses the CSV from disk, ~3 s with the new pyarrow path). This is the same per-row pattern S4.1 removed from csv_manager but left behind in this helper. Net waste: roughly 1-2 minutes per transcription run at 2 M rows plus N_shards x (read+loop) in music_detect, scaling linearly with dataset size.
findings.proposed_fix: Vectorize: reuse csv_manager._normalize_path_series (already exists, csv_manager.py:135) on the filepath column, mask with .isin(requested_norms) and pd.to_numeric(...)>0, then dict(zip(...)) on the filtered frame — drop the per-row tqdm. Additionally hoist the duration load out of the per-shard path in music_detect (load once per worker or pass from the parent like distillmos_filter now does). Identical semantics: same normalization function, same positive-duration filter, same key set.
findings.expected_gain: Per call at 2 M rows: ~5-10 s -> <1 s; transcription saves ~30-50 s per run (5 models), music_detect saves (read+loop) per shard which can be minutes on many-shard runs. Scales linearly with rows.
findings.risk: low — same normalize function applied vectorized; pin with a test comparing the returned dict against the loop version on the existing 2 M-row bench fixture.

findings.title: Stage-7 pending scans bypass DirNameCache: per-file stat per model in get_valid_paths, plus 1+5 stats per file in ROVER discovery
locations: src/transcription/transcription.py:283-316 (get_valid_paths: text_sidecar_complete per file, executed once per model => 5 passes)
src/transcription/transcription.py:262-276 (check_consensus: up to 5 more stats + reads per file when consensus_num>0)
src/transcription/rover.py:52-66 (_pending_audio_paths: one stat per audio file)
src/transcription/rover.py:68-96 (_records_for_audio_paths: path_exists per model per file = 5 stats/file in workers)
src/utils/sidecars.py:34-77 (DirNameCache already exists, used only by stages 8-10)
findings.description: Report S4.7 converted the stage 8-10 pending scans to one-scandir-per-directory via DirNameCache, but stage 7 kept the per-file pattern: get_valid_paths stats one sidecar per audio file and runs once per model (5 x 500k = 2.5 M stats per run), and ROVER adds ~500k stats in _pending_audio_paths plus up to 5 path_exists stats per file again inside _records_for_audio_paths. On the production HDD with cold dentry caches, per-file stats are random seeks (~ms class) while a scandir streams a directory in a few reads — this is the same dominant-on-HDD pattern S4.7 measured. Audio chunks cluster ~50-500 per episode directory, so the syscall count drops by 2-3 orders of magnitude.
findings.proposed_fix: Thread a DirNameCache through get_valid_paths and ROVER: one cache instance per scan, sidecar existence = cache.exists(p.with_name(...)); for retry_empty (needs st_size) keep the stat only for names the cache says exist (rare). In _records_for_audio_paths, transcripts live next to the audio, so the same per-directory name set answers all 5 models at once. Equivalence argument is the same one already validated for S4.7 (identical pending sets on a 50k fixture).
findings.expected_gain: Stage-7 startup scans: 3-5 M stats -> ~one scandir per episode dir (thousands). Warm cache: tens of seconds saved per run at 500k files; cold HDD: minutes-to-hours class, same as the S4.7 measurement rationale.
findings.risk: low — mechanism already proven in S4.7; pin with the same identical-pending-set test on a fixture tree. retry_empty path needs care (size check), keep a stat fallback for it.

findings.title: DistillMOS sort_by_length probes every file serially with torchaudio.info and races on a shared JSON cache, ignoring durations already in balalaika.csv
locations: src/utils/datasets/separation.py:78-116 (sort_by_length: per-call JSON cache, json.dump not atomic, shared across workers)
src/utils/datasets/separation.py:125 (called inside create_distillmos_dataloader => once per worker per shard)
findings.description: Verified still unapplied (was flagged in separation-music-distillmos.json). On a cold cache every DistillMOS worker serially header-probes each file of its shard before any GPU work starts (~3-10 ms per file on HDD => ~25-80 min for 500k files), blocking the GPU. All workers share one distillmos_sorted_files.json keyed by full path lists; concurrent json.dump from multiple workers is non-atomic (a reader can see a truncated file) and the partial-hit path rewrites the entire JSON per shard. Meanwhile total_duration for every file already exists in balalaika.csv (written by the crest stage) and music_detect already consumes it via ensure_audio_durations — the probe is fully redundant data.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace estimate_audio_lengths with ensure_audio_durations (CSV-backed, parallel probe only for genuinely missing rows) and sort by those durations; drop the JSON cache or at minimum write it atomically (tmp+rename) under a lock. Combine with finding #5 so the CSV is read once per worker, not per shard.
findings.expected_gain: Cold-start: minutes-to-an-hour of serial HDD probing removed per run; steady-state: removes a per-shard subset-check + possible whole-JSON rewrite. Also removes a real corrupt-cache race.
findings.risk: medium — sort order is only a batching heuristic, but changing the duration source can reorder ties and change batch composition; pad_sequence padding then differs, and DistillMOS scores on padded batches are not provably invariant to batch composition. Gate behind a config knob and A/B the 250-file bench sample scores (report S5 harness exists), or keep byte-conservative by sorting on identical values where both sources agree.

areas.area_summary: Cross-cutting sweep of all of src/ for compute kernels and leftover per-element Python loops, with numba 0.65.1 confirmed installed in .dev_venv. Read end-to-end: rover.py + the installed crowdkit ROVER, sortformer_onnx.py, preprocess.py timeline/metrics/cut paths, audio_postprocessing.py + installed pyloudnorm, crest_factor_remover.py, all utils/datasets/* loaders, transcription.py, punctuation/accents/fast_g2p, distillmos/music_detect/antispoofing/denoising, collate.py, report.py, audio_durations.py, sidecars.py. The previously optimized layers (CSV manager, collate consistency, webdataset, G2P, RUPunct batching, crest streaming stats) check out as done in current code and were not re-reported. The remaining hot Python concentrates in two third-party pure-Python kernels the pipeline inherits (crowdkit's ROVER alignment DP — the biggest unclaimed win, ~30-50 ms/file of object-heavy Python; pyloudnorm's gating block loop with 4x redundant squaring, fixable bitwise-identically), in Sortformer's CPU post-processing (report S9.2 leads refined here with numba-vs-numpy verdicts and a note that the kernels ARE benchmarkable on this node with synthetic arrays despite the missing .onnx), and in a few leftover per-row/per-file scan loops (audio_durations CSV walk run 5x per transcription run and per-shard in music_detect; stage-7/ROVER pending scans still doing per-file stats despite DirNameCache existing; distillmos sort_by_length serially probing files whose durations are already in balalaika.csv, with a racy shared JSON cache). Numba verdict overall: it clearly wins only for the ROVER DP (data-dependent recurrence) and for bit-exact preservation of Sortformer's sequential float32 running mean and the onset!=offset hysteresis; everything else is better served by plain numpy vectorization or searchsorted-style algorithmic fixes, so no broad numba adoption is warranted. No CUDA-kernel candidates found: GPU-adjacent loops are already batched, SmartVAD already uses DLPack IOBinding, and the remaining GPU items (SmartVAD cross-segment batching, Sortformer IOBinding, shared ASR decode) are report S9 items not re-reported here. Minor still-open trivia confirmed against current code but left to their owning areas: report.py _read_csv_missing_summary per-cell csv.DictReader loop (~24M iterations at 2M rows, already in denoising-export.json), recovery_from_meta.py iterrows (tiny meta files), distillmos_filter 10-bin histogram loop, process_token/format_timestamps (model-dominated), and the per-segment timeline builders in preprocess.py (linear and short).
